Q 89

The angles of a triangle are arranged in descending order of their magnitudes. If the difference between two consecutive angles is {tex}10^{\circ}{/tex}, find the three angles.

Solution

Let ABC be the triangle, and its angles A, B, and c are in descending order.
Now,
{tex}\angle{/tex}A - {tex}\angle{/tex}B = {tex}10^{\circ}{/tex}  ... (i)
{tex}\angle{/tex}B - {tex}\angle{/tex}C = {tex}10^{\circ}{/tex}  ... (ii)
We know that,
{tex}\angle{/tex}A + {tex}\angle{/tex}B + {tex}\angle{/tex}C = {tex}180^{\circ}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}B + {tex}10^{\circ}{/tex} + {tex}\angle{/tex}B + {tex}\angle{/tex}B - {tex}10^{\circ}{/tex} = {tex}180^{\circ}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3{tex}\angle{/tex}B = {tex}180^{\circ}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}B = {tex}\frac{180^{\circ}}{3}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}B = {tex}60^{\circ}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}A = {tex}\angle{/tex}B + {tex}10^{\circ}{/tex} = {tex}60^{\circ}{/tex} + {tex}10^{\circ}{/tex} = {tex}70^{\circ}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}C = {tex}\angle{/tex}B - {tex}10^{\circ}{/tex} = {tex}60^{\circ}{/tex} - {tex}10^{\circ}{/tex} = {tex}50^{\circ}{/tex}

Q 90

In {tex}\Delta{/tex} ABC, DE || BC Figure Find the values of x, y and z.

Solution

From the given figure, we have,
DE parallel to BC and AB is a transversal.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}ADE = {tex}\angle{/tex}DBC
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex}30^{\circ}{/tex}
Now, DE parallel to BC and AC is a transversal.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}AED = {tex}\angle{/tex}ECB
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = {tex}40^{\circ}{/tex}
In triangle ABC, we have,
{tex}\angle{/tex}A + {tex}\angle{/tex}B + {tex}\angle{/tex}C = {tex}180^{\circ}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} z + {tex}30^{\circ}{/tex} + {tex}40^{\circ}{/tex} = {tex}180^{\circ}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} z = {tex}180^{\circ}{/tex} - {tex}40^{\circ}{/tex} - {tex}30^{\circ}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} z = {tex}110^{\circ}{/tex}
Therefore,
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex}30^{\circ}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = {tex}40^{\circ}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} z = {tex}110^{\circ}{/tex}

Q 91

In Figure find the values of x, y and z.

Solution


In triangle ABD, we have,
{tex}\angle{/tex}BAD + {tex}\angle{/tex}BDA + {tex}\angle{/tex}ABD = {tex}180^{\circ}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}60^{\circ}{/tex} + x + {tex}60^{\circ}{/tex} = {tex}180^{\circ}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex}180^{\circ}{/tex} - {tex}120^{\circ}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex}60^{\circ}{/tex}
Now,
{tex}\angle{/tex}ADC = {tex}\angle{/tex}BAD + {tex}\angle{/tex}ABD = {tex}180^{\circ}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = {tex}60^{\circ}{/tex} + {tex}60^{\circ}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = {tex}120^{\circ}{/tex}
Also, in triangle ADC, we have,
{tex}\angle{/tex}ADB = {tex}\angle{/tex}DAC + {tex}\angle{/tex}DCA
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex}30^{\circ}{/tex} + z
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} z = {tex}60^{\circ}{/tex} - {tex}30^{\circ}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} z = {tex}30^{\circ}{/tex}
Therefore,
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex}60^{\circ}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = {tex}120^{\circ}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} z = {tex}30^{\circ}{/tex}

Q 92

If one angle of a triangle is {tex}60^{\circ}{/tex} and the other two angles are in the ratio 1 : 2, find the angles.

Solution

Let {tex}\Delta{/tex} ABC be the given triangle such that {tex}\angle{/tex}A = {tex}60^{\circ}{/tex} and let angles B and C are x and 2x respectively.
{tex}\angle{/tex}A + {tex}\angle{/tex}B + {tex}\angle{/tex}C = {tex}180^{\circ}{/tex}   [Angle sum property]

{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}60^{\circ}{/tex} + x + 2x = {tex}180^{\circ}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3x = {tex}180^{\circ}{/tex} - {tex}60^{\circ}{/tex} = {tex}120^{\circ}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex}\frac{120^{\circ}}{3}{/tex} = {tex}40^{\circ}{/tex}
Therefore, {tex}\angle{/tex}B = {tex}40^{\circ}{/tex} and {tex}\angle{/tex}C = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}40^{\circ}{/tex} = {tex}80^{\circ}{/tex}

Q 93

In {tex}\Delta{/tex} PQR, if 3{tex}\angle{/tex}P = 4{tex}\angle{/tex}Q = 6{tex}\angle{/tex}R, calculate the angles of the triangle.

Solution

In triangle PQR,
{tex}\angle{/tex}P + {tex}\angle{/tex}Q + {tex}\angle{/tex}R = {tex}180^{\circ}{/tex} [Angle sum property]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}  {tex}\frac{6}{3}{/tex}{tex}\angle{/tex}R + {tex}\frac{6}{3}{/tex}{tex}\angle{/tex}R + {tex}\angle{/tex}R = {tex}180^{\circ}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2{tex}\angle{/tex}R + {tex}\frac{3}{2}{/tex}{tex}\angle{/tex}R + {tex}\angle{/tex}R = {tex}180^{\circ}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{(4+3+2)}{2}{/tex}{tex}\angle{/tex}R = {tex}180^{\circ}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{9}{2}{/tex}{tex}\angle{/tex}R = {tex}180^{\circ}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}R = {tex}\frac{180^{\circ} \times 2}{9}{/tex} = {tex}40^{\circ}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}P = {tex}\frac{6}{3}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}40^{\circ}{/tex} = {tex}80^{\circ}{/tex}
and {tex}\angle{/tex}Q = {tex}\frac{6}{3}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}40^{\circ}{/tex} = {tex}60^{\circ}{/tex}
Therefore,
{tex}\angle{/tex}P = {tex}80^{\circ}{/tex}, {tex}\angle{/tex}R = {tex}40^{\circ}{/tex}, {tex}\angle{/tex}Q = {tex}60^{\circ}{/tex}

Q 94

In {tex}\Delta{/tex} DEF, {tex}\angle{/tex}D = {tex}60^{\circ}{/tex}, {tex}\angle{/tex}E = {tex}70^{\circ}{/tex} and the bisectors of {tex}\angle{/tex}E and {tex}\angle{/tex}F meet at O. Find

  1. {tex}\angle{/tex}F
  2. {tex}\angle{/tex}EOF.
Solution
  1. In triangle DEF,
    {tex}\angle{/tex}D + {tex}\angle{/tex}E + {tex}\angle{/tex}F = {tex}180^{\circ}{/tex} [Angle sum property]
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}60^{\circ}{/tex} + {tex}70^{\circ}{/tex} + {tex}\angle{/tex}F = {tex}180^{\circ}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}F = {tex}180^{\circ}{/tex} - {tex}60^{\circ}{/tex} - {tex}70^{\circ}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}F = {tex}50^{\circ}{/tex}
  2. EO and FO are the bisectors of angle E and F respectively.

    {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}OEF = {tex}\angle{/tex}OED = {tex}\frac{70^{\circ}}{2}{/tex} = {tex}35^{\circ}{/tex} and
    {tex}\angle{/tex}OFE = {tex}\angle{/tex}OFD = {tex}\frac{50^{\circ}}{2}{/tex} = {tex}25^{\circ}{/tex}
    Now, in triangle OEF,
    {tex}\angle{/tex}OEF + {tex}\angle{/tex}OFE + {tex}\angle{/tex}EOF = {tex}180^{\circ}{/tex} [Angel Sum Property]
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}35^{\circ}{/tex} + {tex}25^{\circ}{/tex} + {tex}\angle{/tex}EOF = {tex}180^{\circ}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}EOF = {tex}180^{\circ}{/tex} - {tex}35^{\circ}{/tex} - {tex}25^{\circ}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}EOF = {tex}120^{\circ}{/tex}
Q 95

In Figure {tex}\Delta{/tex} PQR is right-angled at P. U and T are the points on line QRF. If QP || ST and US || RP, find {tex}\angle{/tex}S.

Solution

From the figure, we have,
If QP II ST and QT is a transversal, then {tex}\angle{/tex}PQR = {tex}\angle{/tex}STU   [alternate interior angles]
and if DS || RP and QT is a transversal, then {tex}\angle{/tex}PRQ = {tex}\angle{/tex}SUT  [alternate interior angles]
Hence, {tex}\angle{/tex}S must be epual to {tex}\angle{/tex}P i.e. {tex}90^{\circ}{/tex}

Q 96

In Figure if RP = RQ, find the value of x

Solution

Given that RP = RQ
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}RQP = {tex}\angle{/tex}RPQ = x    [angles opposite to equal sides are equal]
Now, {tex}\angle{/tex}RPQ = {tex}50^{\circ}{/tex} [Vertically opposite angles]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} x = {tex}50^{\circ}{/tex}

Q 97

In Figure if ST = SU, then find the values of x and y.

Solution

From the given figure,
{tex}\angle{/tex}TSU = {tex}78^{\circ}{/tex}   [Vertically opposite angle]
{tex}\angle{/tex}SUT = {tex}\angle{/tex}STU = Y
Now, in triangle STU,
{tex}\angle{/tex}SUT + {tex}\angle{/tex}STU + {tex}\angle{/tex}TSU = {tex}180^{\circ}{/tex}
y + y + {tex}78^{\circ}{/tex} = {tex}180^{\circ}{/tex}
2y = {tex}180^{\circ}{/tex} - {tex}78^{\circ}{/tex}
y = {tex}\frac{102^{\circ}}{2}{/tex}
y = {tex}51^{\circ}{/tex}
Now,
x = {tex}\angle{/tex}TSU + {tex}\angle{/tex}UTS
x = {tex}78^{\circ}{/tex} + {tex}51^{\circ}{/tex}
x = {tex}129^{\circ}{/tex}
Therefore,
x = {tex}129^{\circ}{/tex} and, y = {tex}51^{\circ}{/tex}

Q 98

Check whether the following measures (in cm) can be the sides of a right-angled triangle or not.
1.5, 3.6, 3.9

Solution

Here,
(1.5)2 + (3.6)2 = 2.25 + 12.96 = 15.21
and (3.9)2 = 15.21
Thus, (1.5)2 + (3.6)2 = (3.9)2
Which satisfies the Pythagoras theorem.
Therefore, given sides are sides of a right angled triangle.

Q 99

Height of a pole is 8 m. Find the length of rope tied with its top from a point on the ground at a distance of 6 m from its bottom.

Solution

Let ABC be the right-angled triangle

By using Pythagoras theorem,
(AC)2 = 82 + 62
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} AC = {tex}\sqrt{64+36}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} AC = {tex}\sqrt{100}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} AC = 10 m
Hence, the length of the rope is 10 m.

Q 100

In Figure if y is five times x, find the value of z.

Solution

In triangle QRS,
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x + y + {tex}60^{\circ}{/tex} = {tex}180^{\circ}{/tex} [Sum of angles of a triangle is 180o]
It has been given that y = 5x
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x + 5x = {tex}180^{\circ}{/tex} - {tex}60^{\circ}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex}\frac{120^{\circ}}{6}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex}20^{\circ}{/tex}
Thus, y = 5x = 5 ({tex}20^{\circ}{/tex}) = {tex}100^{\circ}{/tex}
Now, by exterior angle property,
{tex}\angle{/tex}RQP = {tex}\angle{/tex}QRS + {tex}\angle{/tex}QSR
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} z = {tex}60^{\circ}{/tex} + {tex}100^{\circ}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} z = {tex}160^{\circ}{/tex}

Q 101

The lengths of two sides of an isosceles triangle are 9 cm and 20 cm. What is the perimeter of the triangle? Give reason.

Solution

Given that, the lengths of two sides of an isosceles triangle are 9 cm and 20 cm.
Thus, the third side must be 20 cm, because the sum of two sides should be greater than the third side.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Perimeter of the triangle
= (9 + 20 + 20) cm
= 49 cm

Q 102

Points A and B are on the opposite edges of a pond as shown in Figure To find the distance between the two points, the surveyor makes a right-angled triangle as shown. Find the distance AB.

Solution


since, {tex}\Delta{/tex} ADC is right-angled triangle
{tex}\therefore{/tex} (AC)2 = (AD)2 + (CD)2
= (30)2 + (40)2 = 900 + 1600 = 2500 = (50)2
{tex}\therefore{/tex} AC = 50 m
Now, AB = AC - BC = 50 - 12 = 38
Thus, the distance AB is 38 m.

Q 103

Two poles of 10 m and 15 m stand upright on a plane ground. If the distance between the tops is 13 m, find the distance between their feet.

Solution

Let AB and CD are the poles of heights 15 m and 10 m such that AC is 13 m.

Now,
CE = BD = 10 m
CD = BE = 10 m
{tex}\therefore{/tex} AE = AB - BE = 15 - 10 = 5 m
Now, in triangle AEC,
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (13)2 = 52 + (EC)2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (EC)2 = 169 - 25
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} EC = {tex}\sqrt{144}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} EC = 12
{tex}\therefore{/tex} BD = 12 m
Therefore, 12 meters is the distance between the feet of the poles.

Q 104

The foot of a ladder is 6 m away from its wall and its top reaches a window 8 m above the ground,

  1. Find the length of the ladder.
  2. If the ladder is shifted in such a way that its foot is 8 m away from the wall, to what height does its top reach?
Solution

  1. Let AC be the given ladder such that BC = 6 m and AB = 8 m
    Now, in triangle ABC
    (AC)2 = (AB)2 + (BC)2
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (AC)2 = 82 + 62
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} AC = {tex}\sqrt{64+36}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} AC = {tex}\sqrt{100}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} AC = 10 m
    Therefore, 10 meters is the length of the ladder.
  2. Let AC be the ladder's length 10 m and BC = 8 m

    Now, in triangle ABC
    (AC)2 = (AB)2 + (BC)2
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (AC)2 = (AB)2 + 82
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (10)2 - 64 = (AB)2
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} AB = {tex}\sqrt{36}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} AB = 6 m
    Therefore, the ladder is 6 meters above the ground.

Q 12

Q 16

In Figure AB = AD and {tex}\angle{/tex}BAC = {tex}\angle{/tex}DAC. Then

  1. {tex}\Delta{/tex} ________ {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} ABC.
  2. BC = ________.
  3. {tex}\angle{/tex}BCA = ________.
  4. Line segment AC bisects ________ and ________.

Solution

In {tex}\triangle{/tex} ABC and {tex}\triangle{/tex} ADC, we have
AC = AC    ...Common
{tex}\angle{/tex} BAC = {tex}\angle{/tex} DAC   ...Given
AB = AD     ...Given
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} ABC = {tex}\Delta{/tex} ADC    ...SAS criterion
BC = DC    ...By C.P.C.T
{tex}\angle{/tex} BCA = {tex}\angle{/tex} DCA   ...By C.P.C.T

  1. {tex}\Delta{/tex} ADC {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} ABC
  2. BC = DC
  3. {tex}\angle{/tex} BCA = {tex}\angle{/tex} DCA
  4. Line AC bisects {tex}\angle{/tex} BAD and {tex}\angle{/tex} BCD

Q 39

In each of the given pairs of triangles of Figure applying only ASA congruence criterion, determine which triangles are congruent. Also, write the congruent triangles in symbolic form.

Solution
  1. In the figure, triangle ABC and PQR are not congruent.
  2. In triangle ABD and CDB
    BD = BD   [Common]
    {tex}\angle{/tex}ABD = {tex}\angle{/tex}CDB = {tex}30^{\circ}{/tex}    [given]
    {tex}\angle{/tex}ADB = {tex}\angle{/tex}CBD = {tex}40^{\circ}{/tex}    [given]
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} ABD {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} CDB    [By ASA Criterion]
  3. In triangle XYZ and LMN
    XY = LM = 4.8    [Common]
    {tex}\angle{/tex}YXZ = {tex}\angle{/tex}MLN = {tex}100^{\circ}{/tex}    [given]
    {tex}\angle{/tex}XYZ = {tex}\angle{/tex}LMN = {tex}50^{\circ}{/tex}     [given]
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} XYZ {tex}\cong{/tex}{tex}\Delta{/tex} LMN    [By ASA criterion]
  4. In triangle ABC and DFE
    {tex}\angle{/tex}A = {tex}\angle{/tex}D    [given]
    {tex}\angle{/tex}B = {tex}\angle{/tex}F     [given]
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}C = {tex}\angle{/tex}E
    BC = FE    [given]
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} ABC {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} DFE   [By ASA criterion]
  5. In triangle PON and MNO
    ON = NO  [Common]
    {tex}\angle{/tex}NOP = {tex}\angle{/tex}ONM = {tex}90^{\circ}{/tex}   [Common]
    {tex}\angle{/tex}ONP = {tex}\angle{/tex}NOM = {tex}50^{\circ}{/tex}   [given]
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} PON {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} MNO  [ By ASA criterion]
  6. In triangle AOD and BOC
    {tex}\angle{/tex}AOD = {tex}\angle{/tex}BOC   [Vertically opposite angles]
    OD = OC   [Given]
    {tex}\angle{/tex}ADO = {tex}\angle{/tex}BCO   [given]
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} AOD {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} BOC  [ By ASA criterion]
Q 40

In each of the given pairs of triangles of Figure using only RHS congruence criterion, determine which pairs of triangles are congruent. In case of congruence, write the result in symbolic form:

Solution
  1. In triangle ADB and ADC
    AD = AD  [Common]
    {tex}\angle{/tex}ADB = {tex}\angle{/tex}ADC = {tex}90^{\circ}{/tex}
    AB = AC   [Hypotenuse]
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} ADB {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} ADC  [RHS criterion]
  2. In triangle XYZ and UZY
    {tex}\angle{/tex}Y = {tex}\angle{/tex}Z = {tex}90^{\circ}{/tex}   [given]
    XZ = UY   [Hypotenuse]
    YZ = ZY   [Common]
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} XYZ {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} UZY  [RHS criterion]
  3. In triangle ACE and BDE
    AC parallel to BD and alternate interior angles.
    {tex}\angle{/tex}ACE = {tex}\angle{/tex}BDE
    CE = DE   [given]
    {tex}\angle{/tex}ACE = {tex}\angle{/tex}BED   [Vertically opposite angles]
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} ACE {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} BDE  [ASA criterion]
    Triangles are not congruent with the RHS congruence criterion but it is congruent.
  4. In triangle ABC
    Using Pythagoras theorem,
    (AC)2 = (AB)2 + (BC)2
    = 62 + 82
    = 36 + 64
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} AC = {tex}\sqrt{100}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} AC = 10 cm
    In triangle EDC,
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} DC = BD - BC
    = (14 - 8) cm = 6 cm
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} DC = 6 cm
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} CE = 10 cm
    Now, In triangle ABC and CDE,
    {tex}\angle{/tex}B = {tex}\angle{/tex}D = {tex}90^{\circ}{/tex}    [given]
    AB = CD = 6 cm
    AC = CE = 10 cm   [Hypotenuse]
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} ABC {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} CDE  [RHS criterion]
  5. Triangle XYZ and YXU are not congruent with any criterion.
  6. In triangle AOD and BOC
    LO = LO  [Common]
    LM = LN = 8 cm   [Hypotenuse]
    {tex}\angle{/tex}LOM = {tex}\angle{/tex}LON = {tex}90^{\circ}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} LOM {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} LON  [RHS criterion]
Q 41

Without drawing the triangles write all six pairs of equal measures in each of the following pairs of congruent triangles.

  1. {tex}\Delta{/tex} STU {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} DEF
  2. {tex}\Delta{/tex} ABC {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} LMN
  3. {tex}\Delta{/tex} YZX {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} PQR
  4. {tex}\Delta{/tex} XYZ {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} MLN
Solution

We know that corresponding parts of congruent triangles are equal.

  1. {tex}\Delta{/tex} STU {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} DEF
    {tex}\angle{/tex}S = {tex}\angle{/tex}D, {tex}\angle{/tex}T = {tex}\angle{/tex}E and {tex}\angle{/tex}U = {tex}\angle{/tex}F
    and sides, ST = DE, TU = EF and SU = DF
  2. {tex}\Delta{/tex} ABC {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} LMN
    {tex}\angle{/tex}A = {tex}\angle{/tex}L, {tex}\angle{/tex}B = {tex}\angle{/tex}M and {tex}\angle{/tex}C = {tex}\angle{/tex}N
    and sides, AB = LM, BC = MN and AC = LN
  3. {tex}\Delta{/tex} YZX {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} PQR
    {tex}\angle{/tex}Y = {tex}\angle{/tex}P, {tex}\angle{/tex}Z = {tex}\angle{/tex}Q and {tex}\angle{/tex}X = {tex}\angle{/tex}R
    and sides, YZ = PQ, ZX = QR and YX = PR
  4. {tex}\Delta{/tex} XYZ {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} MLN
    {tex}\angle{/tex}X = {tex}\angle{/tex}M, {tex}\angle{/tex}Y = {tex}\angle{/tex}L and {tex}\angle{/tex}Z = {tex}\angle{/tex}N
    and sides, XY = ML,YZ = LN and XZ = MN
Q 42

In the following pairs of triangles of Figure the lengths of the sides are indicated along the sides. By applying SSS congruence criterion, determine which triangles are congruent. If congruent, write the results in symbolic form.

Solution
  1. In triangle ABC and NLM,
    Given,
    NL = AB = 5 cm
    BC = LM = 6 cm
    AC = NM = 4 cm
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} ABC {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} NLM  ...SSS criterion
  2. In triangle LMN and GHI,
    Given,
    LM = GH = 4.5 cm
    MN = HI = 6 cm
    MN = GI = 5 cm
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} LMN {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} GHI  ...SSS criterion
  3. In triangle LMN and LON,
    LM = LO = 5 cm
    MN = ON = 5.5 cm
    LN = LN    [Common]
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} LMN {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} LON  ...SSS criterion
  4. In triangle XYZ and YXW,
    Given,
    XY = YX   [Common]
    ZY = WX = 3 cm
    ZX = WY = 5 cm
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} XYZ {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} YXW  ...SSS criterion
  5. In triangle AOB and DOE,
    Given,
    AO = DO = 2 cm
    DE = AE = 2 cm
    EO = BO = 1.5 cm
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} AOB {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} DOE  ...SSS criterion
  6. In triangle STU and SVU,
    Given,
    SU = SU
    ST = SV = 5 cm
    UT = UV = 3 cm
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} SVU {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} STU  ...SSS criterion
  7. In triangle PQR and RSP,
    Given,
    PR = RP
    PQ = RS = 5 cm
    OR = SP = 3 cm
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} PQR {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} RSP  ...SSS criterion
  8. In triangle STU,
    By Pythagoras theorem,
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (TU)2 = (10.5)2 - 52
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (TU)2 = 135.25
    Now, in triangle PQR,
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (QR)2 = (10.5)2 - 52
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (QR)2 = 135.25
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} (QR)2 = (TU)2
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (QR) = (TU)  ...(i)
    In triangle STU and POR,
    ST = PQ = 5 cm
    SU = PR = 10.5 cm
    QR = TU   from (i)
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} STU {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} PQR ...SSS criterion
Q 43

ABC is an isosceles triangle with AB = AC and D is the mid-point of base BC in the figure below.

  1. State three pairs of equal parts in the triangles ABD and ACD.
  2. Is {tex}\Delta{/tex} ABD {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} ACD. If so why?

Solution
  1. In triangle ABD and ACD,
    AD = AD
    AB = AC
    BD = CD  [D is mid-point at BC]
  2. By using part a of the solution, we get,
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} ABD {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} ACD  by SSS criterion.
Q 44

In Figure it is given that LM = ON and NL = MO

  1. State the three pairs of equal parts in the triangles NOM and MLN.
  2. Is {tex}\Delta{/tex} NOM {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} MLN. Give reason?

Solution
  1. In triangle MLN and NOM,
    NM = MN
    LN = OM
    LM = ON
  2. By using part (a) of the solution, we get,
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} MLN {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} NOM by SSS criterion.
Q 45

Triangles DEF and LMN are both isosceles with DE = DF and LM = LN, respectively. If DE = LM and EF = MN, then, are the two triangles congruent? Which condition do you use?
If {tex}\angle{/tex}E = {tex}40^{\circ}{/tex}, what is the measure of {tex}\angle{/tex}N?

Solution


In triangle LMN and DEF,
MN = EF
LM = DE
LN = DF
{tex}\because{/tex} [DE = DF and LM = LN]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} LMN {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} DEF by SSS critrerion.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}E = {tex}\angle{/tex}M   (by C.P.C.T)
Now,
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}E = {tex}40^{\circ}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}M = {tex}40^{\circ}{/tex}
{tex}\because{/tex} LN = LM
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}N = {tex}40^{\circ}{/tex}

Q 46

If {tex}\Delta{/tex} PQR and {tex}\Delta{/tex} SQR are both isosceles triangle on a common base QR such that P and S lie on the same side of QR. Are triangles PSQ and PSR congruent? Which condition do you use?

Solution

Let triangle SQR and PQR are the given triangles such that PR = PQ and SR = SQ.

In triangle PSR and PSQ,
PR = PQ
SR = SQ
PS = PS
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} PSR {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} PSQ  by SSS criterion.
Therefore, both triangles PSR and PSQ are congruent with the SSS criterion.

Q 47

In Figure which pairs of triangles are congruent by SAS congruence criterion (condition)? If congruent, write the congruence of the two triangles in symbolic form.

Solution
  1. In triangle PQR and TUS,
    Given,
    TU = PQ = 3 cm
    US = OR = 5.5 cm
    {tex}\angle{/tex}POR = {tex}\angle{/tex}TUS = {tex}40^{\circ}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} TUS {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} POR ...SAS criterion
  2. Both triangle JKL and MNO are not congruent with any criterion,
  3. In triangle ABE and CBD,
    {tex}\angle{/tex}EAB = {tex}\angle{/tex}DCB = {tex}50^{\circ}{/tex}
    CD = AE = 5 cm
    CB = AB = 5.2 cm
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} CBD {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} ABE ...SAS criterion
  4. In triangle SUT and XYZ,
    Given,
    XZ = ST
    YZ = UT = 4 cm
    {tex}\angle{/tex}XZY = {tex}\angle{/tex}STU = {tex}30^{\circ}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} XZY {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} SUT ...SAS criterion
  5. In triangle DOF and HOC,
    Given,
    HO = DO
    CO = FO
    {tex}\angle{/tex}DOF = {tex}\angle{/tex}HOC  [Vertical opposite angle]
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} HOC {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} DOF ...SAS criterion
  6. Both triangles ABC and DEF are not congruent with any criterion
  7. In triangle PSQ and RQS,
    Given,
    QS = SQ
    PS = RQ = 4 cm
    {tex}\angle{/tex}PSQ = {tex}\angle{/tex}RQS = {tex}40^{\circ}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} PSQ {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} RQS ...SAS criterion
  8. In triangle LMN and OMN,
    Given,
    NM = MN
    LM = OM
    {tex}\angle{/tex}LMN = {tex}\angle{/tex}OMN = {tex}40^{\circ}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} OMN {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} LMN ...SAS criterion
Q 48

State which of the following pairs of triangles are congruent. If yes, write them in symbolic form (you may draw a rough figure).

  1. {tex}\Delta{/tex} PQR : PQ = 3.5 cm, QR = 4.0 cm, {tex}\angle{/tex}Q = {tex}60^{\circ}{/tex}
    {tex}\Delta{/tex} STU : ST = 3.5 cm, TU = 4 cm, {tex}\angle{/tex}T = {tex}60^{\circ}{/tex}
  2. {tex}\Delta{/tex} ABC : AB = 4.8 cm, {tex}\angle{/tex}A = {tex}90^{\circ}{/tex}, AC = 6.8 cm
    {tex}\Delta{/tex} XYZ : YZ = 6.8 cm, {tex}\angle{/tex}X = {tex}90^{\circ}{/tex}, ZX = 4.8 cm
Solution
  1. In triangle PQR and STU,
    Given,
    PQ = ST = 3.5 cm
    {tex}\angle{/tex}PQR = {tex}\angle{/tex}STU = {tex}60^{\circ}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} OR = TU = 4 cm
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} STU {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} PQR    .........SAS criterion
  2. Both triangles ABC and XYZ are not congruent with any criterion.
Q 49

In Figure below, PQ = PS and {tex}\angle{/tex}1 = {tex}\angle{/tex}2.

  1. Is {tex}\Delta{/tex} PQR {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} PSR? Give reasons.
  2. Is QR = SR? Give reasons.

Solution

From the given figure, we have, 

  1. In triangles PSR and PQR,
    Given,
    PS = PQ
    {tex}\angle{/tex}1 = {tex}\angle{/tex}2
    PR = PR
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} PSR {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} POR by SAS criterion
  2. Yes, SR = QR by C.P.C.T
Q 50

In Figure below, DE = IH, EG = FI and {tex}\angle{/tex}E = {tex}\angle{/tex}I. Is {tex}\Delta{/tex} DEF {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} HIG? If yes, by which congruence criterion?

Solution

In triangle HIG and DEF,
Given,
HI = DE
{tex}\angle{/tex}E = {tex}\angle{/tex}I
EF = IG
{tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} \because \mathrm{IF}=\mathrm{EG} \\ \because \mathrm{IF}+\mathrm{GF}=\mathrm{EG}+\mathrm{GF} \\ \Rightarrow \mathrm{IG}=\mathrm{EF} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} HIG {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} DEF   ..........SAS criterion

Q 51

In Figure below, {tex}\angle{/tex}1 = {tex}\angle{/tex}2 and {tex}\angle{/tex}3 = {tex}\angle{/tex}4.

  1. Is {tex}\Delta{/tex} ADC {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} ABC? Why?
  2. Show that AD = AB and CD = CB.

Solution
  1. In triangle ABC and ADC,
    Given,
    {tex}\angle{/tex}1 = {tex}\angle{/tex}2
    AC = AC
    {tex}\angle{/tex}3 = {tex}\angle{/tex}4
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} ABC {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} ADC   ........SAS criterion
  2. By using the part (a) of a solution, we get
    {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} \mathrm{AB}=\mathrm{AD} \\ \mathrm{CB}=\mathrm{CD} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
Q 52

Observe Figure and state the three pairs of equal parts in triangles ABC and DBC.

  1. Is {tex}\Delta{/tex} ABC {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} DCB? Why?
  2. Is AB = DC? Why?
  3. Is AC = DB? Why?

Solution
  1. In triangle DCB and ABC,

    Given,
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}ABC = {tex}\angle{/tex}DCB = {tex}40^{\circ}{/tex} + {tex}30^{\circ}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}70^{\circ}{/tex}
    {tex}\angle{/tex}ACB = {tex}\angle{/tex}DBC = {tex}30^{\circ}{/tex}
    CB = BC
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} ABC {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} DCB   .........ASA criterion
  2. By using the part (i) of a solution, we get AB = DC     ..........By C.P.C.T
  3. By using the part (i) of a solution, we get AC = DB     ..........By C.P.C.T
Q 53

In the figure below, QS {tex}\perp{/tex} PR, RT {tex}\perp{/tex} PQ and QS = RT.

  1. Is {tex}\Delta{/tex} QSR {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} RTQ? Give reasons.
  2. Is {tex}\angle{/tex}PQR = {tex}\angle{/tex}PRQ? Give reasons.

Solution
  1. In triangle RTO and QRS,
    Given,
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}RTO = {tex}\angle{/tex}OSR = {tex}90^{\circ}{/tex}   (QS {tex}\perp{/tex} PR and RT {tex}\perp{/tex} PQ)
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} QS = RT
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} QR = RO   ..........Hypotenuse
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} RTQ {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} QSR   ..........RHS criterion
  2. By using the part (i) of a solution, we get
    {tex}\angle{/tex}TQR = {tex}\angle{/tex}SRQ   ............By C.P.C.T
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}PQR = {tex}\angle{/tex}PRQ
Q 54

In Figure below, state the three pairs of equal parts in {tex}\Delta{/tex} ABC and {tex}\Delta{/tex} EOD. Is {tex}\Delta{/tex} ABC {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} EOD? Why?

Solution

In triangle ABC and EOD,
AE = EO     ..........given
{tex}\angle{/tex}ABC = {tex}90^{\circ}{/tex} = {tex}\angle{/tex}EOD   .........given
ED = AC  ..........Hypotenuse
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} ABC {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} EOD   ...........RHS criterion

Q 8

Suhana sells a sofa set for 9600 making a profit of 20%. What is the C.P. of the sofa set?

Solution

Profit = 20% of CP, SP = CP + profit
So, 9600   = CP + 20% of CP 
{tex}=\mathrm{CP}+\frac{20}{100} \times \mathrm{CP}{/tex}
{tex}=\left(1+\frac{1}{5}\right) \mathrm{CP}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{6}{5} \mathrm{cp}{/tex}
Therefore, {tex}9600 \times \frac{5}{6}=\mathrm{CP}{/tex}
or {tex}\mathrm{CP}= ₹ 8000{/tex}

Q 9

John borrowed ₹ 75000 from his friend and after one year returned ₹ 80000 to his friend. Find interest.

Solution

Given that, Principal =  ₹ 75000
and, Amount =  ₹ 80000
Thus, Interest = Amount - Principal
=  ₹ 80000 - ₹ 75000
=  ₹ 5000

Q 10

If Meenakshee pays an interest of  ₹1500 for 4 years on a sum of  ₹2500, find the rate of interest per annum(p.a.)

Solution

Here, P =  ₹ 2500, T = 4 year , I =  ₹ 1500
R = ?
We know that, {tex}\mathrm{I}=\frac{\mathrm{P} \times \mathrm{R} \times \mathrm{T}}{100}{/tex}
Therefore, {tex}1500=\frac{2500 \times R \times 4}{100}{/tex}
R = {tex}\frac{1500 \times 100}{2500 \times 4}=15{/tex}
So, the rate of interest is 15%

Q 11

Refer to the graphic. If a cheetah and tortoise travel at their top speeds for 1 minute; how much farther does the cheetah travel?

Solution

Plan a strategy :

  • Begin by determining the distance travelled by each animal in 1 minute.
  • 1 min = 60 seconds.
  • multiply each top speed (m/s) by 60.
  • Subtract to find the difference between the distances travelled by two animals.

solve :

  • 31.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 60 = 1878 m Distance Cheetah travels in 1 minute
  • 08 {tex}\times{/tex} 60 = 4.8 m Distance tortoise travels in 1 minute
  • 1878 m - 4.8 m = 1873.2 m distance travelled by Cheetah farther than Tortoise in one minute.

Revise :
speed of Cheetah = {tex}\frac{D}{T}=\frac{1878 m}{60 s}=31.3 \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}{/tex}
speed of Tortoise = {tex}\frac{D}{T}=\frac{4.8 m}{60 s}=0.08 m / s{/tex}
Therefore, our answer is correct.

Q 81

Imagine that a 10 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 grid has value 300 and that this value is divided evenly among the small squares. In other words, each small square is worth 3. Use a new grid for each part of this problem, and label each grid “Value : 300.”

  1. Shade 25% of the grid. What is 25% of 300? Compare the two answers.
  2. What is the value of 25 squares?
  3. Shade 17% of the grid. What is 17% of 300? Compare the two answers.
  4. What is the value of {tex}\frac{1}{10}{/tex} of the grid?

Solution



  1. As, Grid contain 100 squares, shade 25 squares in order to shade the 25% of the grid.
    Also, value of 1 square = 3
    Value of 25 squares = 25 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 = 75
    and 25% of 300 = 25 {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{300}{100}{/tex} = 75
    Therefore, Both the quantities are equal.

  2. Value of 1 square = 3
    Value of 25 squares = 25 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 = 75



  3. As, Grid contain 100 squares, Shade 17 squares in order to shade the 17% of the grid.
    Also, value of 1 square = 3
    Value of 17 squares = 17 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 = 51
    and 17% of 300 = 17 {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{300}{100}{/tex} = 51
    Therefore, Both the quantities are equal.

  4. Value of 1 grid = 300
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} Value of {tex}\frac{1}{10}{/tex} grid = 300 {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{10}{/tex}
    = 30

Q 102

Chalk contains 10% calcium, 3% carbon and 12% oxygen. Find the amount of carbon and calcium (in grams) in 2{tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} kg of chalk

= 2.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 1000 = 2500gm

Q 104

In a furniture shop, 24 tables were bought at the rate of {tex}₹{/tex} 450 per table. The shopkeeper sold 16 of them at the rate of {tex}₹{/tex} 600 per table and the remaining at the rate of 400 per table. Find her gain or loss percent

Solution

Given, Cost price per table = 450
Also, number of tables = 24
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} CP of 24 tables = 24 {tex}\times{/tex} 450 = 10800
Now, 16 tables sold at the rate of 600
{tex}\therefore{/tex} SP of 16 tables = 16 {tex}\times{/tex} 600 = 9600
And remaining tables = 24 - 16 = 8
8 tables sold at the rate of 400
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} SP for 8 tables = 8 {tex}\times{/tex} 400 = 3200
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Total SP = 9600 + 3200
= 12800
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Profit or gain = SP - CP
= 12800 - 10800
= 2000
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Gain% = {tex}\frac{\text { Gain }}{\mathrm{CP}} \times{/tex} 100%
= {tex}\frac{2000}{10800} \times{/tex} 100%
= 18.51%

Q 105

Medha deposited 20% of her money in a bank. After spending 20% of the remainder, she has {tex}₹{/tex} 4800 left with her. How much did she originally have?

Solution

{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Let medha has originally x money
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Money deposited in bank = 20% of x = {tex}\frac{20}{100} \times{/tex} x = {tex}\frac{1}{5}{/tex} x
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Remaining money = x - {tex}\frac{1}{5}{/tex} x = {tex}\frac{4}{5}{/tex} x
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Money spent = 20% of remaining money = {tex}\frac{20}{100} \times \frac{4}{5}{/tex} x = {tex}\frac{4}{25}{/tex} x
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Money left = {tex}\frac{4}{5x}{/tex} - {tex}\frac{4}{25}{/tex} x
= {tex}\frac{16}{25}{/tex} x
But given, money = 4800
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{16}{25}{/tex} x = 4800
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex}\frac{(4800 \times 25)}{16}{/tex}
= 7500

Q 106

The cost of a flower vase got increased by 12%. If the current cost is {tex}₹{/tex} 896, what was its original cost?

Solution

{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x + 12% of x = 896
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x + {tex}\frac{12}{100}{/tex} x = 896
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{112 x}{100}{/tex} = 896
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex}\frac{896 \times 100}{112}{/tex}
Therefore, x = 800

Q 107

Radhika borrowed {tex}₹{/tex} 12000 from her friends. Out of which {tex}₹{/tex} 4000 were borrowed at 18% and the remaining at 15% rate of interest per annum. What is the total interest after 3 years?

Solution

For first year interest, we have
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} p1 = 4000, R1 = 18% and T1 = 3year
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I1 = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{P}_{1} \times \mathrm{R} 1 \times \mathrm{T}_{1}}{100}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{4000 \times 18 \times 3}{100}{/tex}
= 2160
For second year
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} P2 = 12000 - 4000 = 8000, R2 = 15% and T2 = 3year
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I2 = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{P} 2 \times \mathrm{R} 2 \times \mathrm{T} 2}{100}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{8000 \times 15 \times 3}{100}{/tex}
= 3600
Hence, after 3 year total interest = I1 + I2 = 2160 + 3600
= 5760

Q 110

Dhruvika invested money for a period from May 2006 to April 2008 at rate of 12% per annum. If interest received by her is {tex}₹{/tex} 1620, find the money invested

Q 111

A person wanted to sell a scooter at a loss of 25%. But at the last moment he changed his mind and sold the scooter at a loss of 20%. If the difference in the two SP’s is {tex}₹{/tex} 4000, then find the CP of the scooter

Solution

Let x be the cost price of the scooter.
After 25% loss, the selling price of the scooter is {tex}\left(\frac{100-25}{100}\right) \times{/tex} x = {tex}\frac{3}{4}{/tex}x
After 20% loss, the selling price of the scooter is {tex}\left(\frac{100-20}{100}\right) \times{/tex} x = {tex}\left(\frac{80}{100}\right){/tex}x = {tex}\frac{4}{5}{/tex}x
Difference in the both selling price of the scooter, = {tex}\frac{4}{5}{/tex}x - {tex}\frac{3}{4}{/tex}x = {tex}\frac{1}{20}{/tex}x
According to question,
{tex}\frac{1}{20}{/tex}x = 4000
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 4000 {tex}\times{/tex} 20 = 80000

Q 113

In an entertainment programme, 250 tickets of {tex}₹{/tex} 400 and 500 tickets of {tex}₹{/tex} 100 were sold. If the entertainment tax is 40% on ticket of {tex}₹{/tex} 400 and 20% on ticket of {tex}₹{/tex} 100, find how much entertainment tax was collected from the programme.

Solution

Given that 250 tickets of Rs 400 were sold.
Thus, the total amount received by selling these tickets = 250 {tex}\times{/tex} 400 = 100000
Amount received by selling 500 tickets of Rs 100 = 500 {tex}\times{/tex} 100 = 50000
Also given that, 40% and 20% of entertainment tax is on Rs 400 and Rs 100 tickets respectively.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Total entertainment tax collected = 40% of 100000 + 20% of 50000
= {tex}\frac{40}{100} \times{/tex} 100000 + {tex}\frac{20}{100} \times{/tex} 50000
= 40000 + 10000
= 50000

Q 114

Bhavya earns {tex}₹{/tex} 50,000 per month and spends 80% of it. Due to pay revision, her monthly income increases by 20% but due to price rise, she has to spend 20% more. Find her new savings

Solution

Given that Bhavya earns 50000 per month
And spends 80% of 50000 = {tex}\frac{80}{100} \times{/tex} 50000 = 40000
And savings per month = 50000 - 40000 = 10000
Given increment in monthly income = 20% of 50000
= {tex}\frac{20}{100} \times{/tex} 50000 = 10000
Bhavya’s new income = 50000 + 10000 = 60000
Increase in expenditure = 20% of 40000 = {tex}\frac{20}{100} \times{/tex} 40000 = 80000
New expenditure = 40000 + 8000 = 48000
Therefore, Bhavya’s new savings = 60000 - 48000 = 12000

Q 115

In an examination, there are three papers each of 100 marks. A candidate obtained 53 marks in the first and 75 marks in the second paper. How many marks must the candidate obtain in the third paper to get an overall of 70 per cent marks?

Solution

Let x be the marks of candidate in thirs paper.
According to question,
Total marks secured in all three papers = 53 + 75 + x
And total marks of three papers = 100 + 100 + 100 = 300
Percentage of marks = {tex}\frac{\text { Total marks secured }}{\text { total marks }} \times{/tex} 100%
= {tex}\frac{53+75+x}{300} \times{/tex} 100%
Overall 70% of marks obtained
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{53+75+x}{300} \times{/tex} 100 = 70
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{128+x}{3}{/tex} = 70
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 128 + x = 210
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 210 - 128
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 82
Hence, 82 marks must be secured to get overall of 70% marks

Q 116

Health Application
A doctor reports blood pressure in millimetres of mercury (mm Hg) as a ratio of systolic blood pressure to diastolic blood pressure (such as 140 over 80). Systolic pressure is measured when the heart beats, and diastolic pressure is measured when it rests. Refer to the table of blood pressure ranges for adults
Manohar is a healthy 37 years old man whose blood pressure is in the normal category.

  1. Calculate an approximate ratio of systolic to diastolic blood pressures in the normal range.
  2. If Manohar’s systolic blood pressure is 102 mm Hg, use the ratio from part (a) to predict his diastolic blood pressure.
  3. Calculate ratio of average systolic to average diastolic blood pressure in the prehypertension category
Blood Pressure Ranges
  Normal Prehypertension Hypertension
(Very High)
Systolic
Diastolic		 Under 120 mm Hg
Under 80 mm Hg		 120-139 mm Hg
80-89 mm Hg		 140 mm Hg and above
90 mm Hg and above
Solution

From the given data, we have, 

  1. Systolic blood pressure in the normal range = 120mm Hg
    Diastolic blood pressure in the normal range = 80mm
    The approximate ratio of systolic to diastolic blood pressure = {tex}\frac{\text { Systolic blodd pressure in normal range }}{\text { Diastolic blood presure in normal range }}{/tex}
    = {tex}\frac{120}{80}{/tex}
    = {tex}\frac{3}{2}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} ratio = 3 : 2

  2. Manohar’s systolic blood pressure = 102mm Hg
    Let diastolic blood pressure = x mm Hg
    The approximate ratio of systolic to diastolic blood pressure = {tex}\frac{\text { Systolic blodd pressure in normal range }}{\text { Diastolic blood presure in normal range }}{/tex}
    = {tex}\frac{102}{x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{3}{2}{/tex}
    x = {tex}\frac{102 \times 2}{3}{/tex}
    x = 68mm Hg

  3. Average systolic blood pressure in prehypertension category = {tex}\frac{120+139}{2}{/tex}
    = {tex}\frac{259}{2}{/tex}mm Hg
    Average diastolic blood pressure in prehypertension category = {tex}\frac{80+89}{2}{/tex}
    = {tex}\frac{169}{2}{/tex}mm Hg
    Hence, ratio of average systolic to average diastolic blood pressure = {tex}\frac{\text { Average systolic blood pressure }}{\text { Average diastolic blood pressure }}{/tex}
    = {tex}\frac{\frac{259}{2}}{\frac{169}{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{259}{2} \times \frac{2}{169}{/tex}
    = {tex}\frac{259}{169}{/tex}
    Ratio is 259 : 169

Q 120

Given below are few Mathematical terms
Find

  2. The percentage of consonants in each of the terms

    = 3 : 2
    In term “Perpendicular” 8 consonants and 5 vowels are there
    Therefore, {tex}\frac{8}{5}{/tex}
    = 8 : 5
    In term “Transversal” 8 consonants and 3 vowels are there
    Therefore, {tex}\frac{8}{3}{/tex}
    = 8 : 3
    In term “Correspondence” 9 consonants and 5 vowels are there
    Therefore, {tex}\frac{9}{5}{/tex}
    = 9 : 5

  2. Percentage of consonants = {tex}\frac{(\text { Number of consonants })}{\text { Total number of letters }} \times{/tex} 100%
    Total number of letters = Number of consonants + number of vowels
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} In term “Hypotenuse” total number of letters = 6 + 4 = 10
    = {tex}\frac{6}{10} \times{/tex} 100% = 60%
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} In term “Congruence” total number of letters = 6 + 4 = 10
    = {tex}\frac{6}{10} \times{/tex} 100% = 60%
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} In term “Perpendicular” total number of letters = 8 + 5 = 13
    = {tex}\frac{8}{13} \times{/tex} 100% = 61.53%
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} In term “Transversal” total number of letters = 8 + 3 = 11
    = {tex}\frac{8}{11} \times{/tex} 100% = 72.72%
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} In term “Correspondence” total number of letters = 9 + 5 = 14
    = {tex}\frac{9}{14} \times{/tex} 100% = 64.28%

Q 121

What’s the Error? An analysis showed that 0.06 per cent of the T-shirts made by one company were defective. A student says this is 6 out of every 100. What is the student’s error?

Solution

Here, 0.06% = {tex}\frac{0.06}{100}=\frac{6}{10000}{/tex}
Therefore, 6 out of 10000 are defective.

Q 122

What’s the Error? A student said that the ratios {tex}\frac{3}{4}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{9}{16}{/tex} were proportional. What error did the student make?

Solution

Here, {tex}\frac{3}{4}=\frac{3 \times 4}{4 \times 4}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{12}{16} \neq \frac{9}{16}{/tex}
Therefore, {tex}\frac{3}{4}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{9}{16}{/tex} are not proportional

Q 123

What’s the Error? A clothing store charges {tex}₹{/tex} 1024 for 4 T-shirts. A student says that the unit price is {tex}₹{/tex} 25.6 per T-shirt. What is the error? What is the correct unit price?

Solution

Given that cost of 4 T-shirts = 1024
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Cost of 1 T-shirt =  {tex}\frac{1024}{4}{/tex} = 256
{tex}\therefore{/tex} The correct unit price = 256

Q 124

A tea merchant blends two varieties of tea in the ratio of 5 : 4. The cost of first variety is {tex}₹{/tex} 200 per kg and that of second variety is {tex}₹{/tex} 300 per kg. If he sells the blended tea at the rate of {tex}₹{/tex} 275 per kg, find out the percentage of her profit or loss

Solution

Let the quantity of first variety and second variety of tea is 5y kg and 4y kg respectively
Cost of first variety of tea = 200 {tex}\times{/tex} 5y = 1000y
Cost of second variety of tea = 300 {tex}\times{/tex} 4y = 1200y
So, total cost price of tea = 1000y + 1200y = 2200y
Here, Selling price of tea = 9y {tex}\times{/tex} 275 = 2475y
Here, S.P. > C.P.
Therefore, the profit = 2475 - 2200 = Rs 275
Thus, Profit% = {tex}\frac{\text { Profit }}{\text { C.P. }} \times{/tex} 100
= {tex}\frac{275 x}{2200 x} \times{/tex} 100
= 12.5%

Q 125

A piece of cloth 5 m long shrinks 10 percent on washing. How long will the cloth be after washing?

Solution

Given that length of shrink cloth = 10% of 5m
= {tex}\frac{10}{100} \times{/tex} 5 = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}m
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Length of cloth after wash = 5 - {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{9}{2}{/tex}
= 4.5m

Q 126

Nancy obtained 426 marks out of 600 and the marks obtained by Rohit are 560 out of 800. Whose performance is better?

Solution

Given that Nancy got 426 marks out of 600.
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Percentage marks = {tex}\frac{426}{600} \times{/tex} 100% = 71%
Also, Rohit got 560 marks out of 800.
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Percentage marks = {tex}\frac{560}{800} \times{/tex} 100% = 70%
Hence, Nancy shows better performance.

Q 127

A memorial trust donates ₹5,00,000 to a school, the interest on which is to be used for awarding 3 scholarships to students obtaining first three positions in the school examination every year. If the donation earns an interest of 12 percent per annum and the values of the second and third scholarships are ₹20,000 and ₹15,000 respectively, find out the value of the first scholarship.

Solution

Given, Donation amount = 500000
And Rate of interest for each year = 12% per annum
Time period = 1 year
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Interest received after 1 year = {tex}\frac{500000 \times 12 \times 1}{100}{/tex} = 5000 {tex}\times{/tex} 12
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Scholarship amount for second position = 20000
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Scholarship amount for third position = 15000
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Remaining amount for first position student = 60000 - (20000 + 15000)
= 25000

Q 128

Ambika got 99 per cent marks in Mathematics, 76 per cent marks in Hindi, 61 per cent in English, 84 per cent in Science, and 95% in Social Science. If each subject carries 100 marks, then find the percentage of marks obtained by Ambika in the aggregate of all the subjects

Solution

Given that each subject carries 100 marks
Thus, Ambika got marks as follows:
Mathematics = 99, Hindi = 76, English = 61, Science = 84 and Social science = 95
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Aggregate marks percentage = {tex}\frac{\text { Marks obtained by ambika }}{\text { Total Marks }} \times{/tex} 100%

Q 129

What sum of money lent out at 16 per cent per annum simple interest would produce {tex}₹{/tex} 9600 as interest in 2 years?

Solution

Given that I = 9600, T = 2 years and R = 16%
We know that, I = {tex}\frac{P \times R \times T}{100}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} P = {tex}\frac{I \times 100}{\mathrm{R} \times \mathrm{T}}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{9600 \times 100}{16 \times 2}{/tex}
= 30000

Q 130

Harish bought a gas-chullah for {tex}₹{/tex} 900 and later sold it to Archana at a profit of 5 per cent. Archana used it for a period of two years and later sold it to Babita at a loss of 20 per cent. For how much did Babita get it?

Solution

Given, Chullah was bought for 900 and sold at a 5% profit.
Cost Price (CP) of Chullah for Archana = 900 + 5% of 900
= 900 + {tex}\frac{5}{100} \times{/tex} 900
= 900 + 45 = 945
Archana sold chullah for Babita at a loss of 20%
Hence, CP of chullah for Babita = 945 - 20% of 945
= 945 - {tex}\frac{20}{100} \times{/tex} 945
= 945 - 189
= 756

Q 132

In a debate competition, the judges decide that 20 per cent of the total marks would be given for accent and presentation. 60 per cent of the rest are reserved for the subject matter and the rest are for rebuttal. If this means 8 marks for rebuttal, then find the total marks

Solution

Let x be the total marks
Marks given for accent and presentation = 20% of x = {tex}\frac{20}{100} \times{/tex} x = {tex}\frac{x}{5}{/tex}
Remaining marks = x - {tex}\frac{x}{5}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{4x}{5}{/tex}
Marks reserved for subject matter = 60% of rest marks = {tex}\frac{60}{100} \times{/tex} {tex}\frac{4x}{5}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{12x}{25}{/tex}
Now, remaining marks = {tex}\frac{4x}{5}{/tex} - {tex}\frac{12x}{5}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{8x}{25}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{8x}{25}{/tex} = 8
Thus, x = 25

Q 133

Divide {tex}₹{/tex} 10000 in two parts so that the simple interest on the first part for 4 years at 12 per cent per annum may be equal to the simple interest on the second part for 4.5 years at 16 per cent per annum

Solution

Given, money = 10000
Money should be divided into two parts, SI on the first part for 4yr at 12% and SI on the second part for 4.5yr at 16%
Let the first part = 7X
Secon part = (10000 - x)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} SI1 = {tex}\frac{P 1 \times R 1 \times T 1}{100}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{x \times 12 \times 4}{100}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} SI2 = {tex}\frac{P 2 \times R 2 \times T 2}{100}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{(10000 - x) \times 16 \times 4.5}{100}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{x \times 12 \times 4}{100}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{(10000 - x) \times 16 \times 4.5}{100}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{48x}{4.5 \times 16}{/tex} = (10000 - x)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{48 x \times 10}{45 \times 16}{/tex} = (10000 - x)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{2}{3}{/tex}x = 10000 - x
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{5x}{3}{/tex} = 10000
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 6000
Therefore, First part = 6000
And, Second part = 10000 - x = 10000 - 6000 = 4000

Q 134

{tex}₹{/tex} 9000 becomes {tex}₹{/tex} 18000 at simple interest in 8 years. Find the rate per cent per annum

Solution

Given that, P = 9000, A = 18000, and T = 8 years.
We know that, A = P + I
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = A - P
= 18000 - 9000
= 9000
We know that, I = {tex}\frac{P \times R \times T}{100}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} R = {tex}\frac{I \times 100}{P \times T}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{9000 \times 100}{9000 \times 8}{/tex}
= 12.5%

Q 135

In how many years will the simple interest on a certain sum be 4.05 times the principal at 13.5 per cent per annum?

Solution

Let Principal = P, R = 13.5%, I = 4.05 times principal = 4.05 {tex}\times{/tex} P
We know that, I = {tex}\frac{P \times R \times T}{100}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 4.05 {tex}\times{/tex} P = {tex}\frac{P \times 13.5 \times T}{100}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} T = {tex}\frac{405P}{P \times 13.5}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{405 \times 10}{135}{/tex}
= 30 year

Q 136

The simple interest on a certain sum for 8 years at 12 per cent per annum is {tex}₹{/tex} 3120 more than the simple interest on the same sum for 5 years at 14 per cent per annum. Find the sum.

Solution

Let the sum = x
Given, P1 = x, R1 = 12% and T1 = 8 year and
P2 = x, R2 = 14% and T2 = 5 year
Also, difference between I1 and I2 is 3120
{tex}\therefore{/tex} I1 - I2 = 3120
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{P_ 1 \times R _1 \times T _1}{100}{/tex} - {tex}\frac{P _2 \times R _2 \times T_ 2}{100}{/tex} = 3120
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{x \times 12 \times 8}{100}{/tex} - {tex}\frac{x \times 14 \times 5}{100}{/tex} = 3120
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 96x - 70x = 3120 {tex}\times{/tex} 100
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 26x = 312000
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 12000
Thus the sum = Rs 12000.

Q 137

The simple interest on a certain sum for 2.5 years at 12 per cent per annum is {tex}₹{/tex} 300 less than the simple interest on the same sum for 4.5 years at 8 per cent per annum. Find the sum.

Solution

Let the sum = x
Given, P1 = x, R1 = 12% and T1 = 2.5year = {tex}\frac{5}{2}{/tex}year and
P2 = x, R2 = 8% and T2 = 4.5 year = {tex}\frac{9}{2}{/tex}year
Also, the difference between I1 and I2 is 300
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I2 - I1 = 300
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{\mathrm{P} _2 \times \mathrm{R} _2 \times \mathrm{T} _2}{100}{/tex} - {tex}\frac{\mathrm{P} _1 \times \mathrm{R} _1 \times \mathrm{T} _1}{100}{/tex} = 300
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{\mathrm{x} \times \mathrm{8} \times \mathrm{9}}{2 \times 100}{/tex} - {tex}\frac{\mathrm{x} \times \mathrm{12} \times \mathrm{5}}{2 \times 100}{/tex} = 300
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 72x - 60x = 300 {tex}\times{/tex} 200
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 12x = 60000
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 5000
Therefore the sum is 5000.

Q 139

150 students are studying English, Maths or both. 62 per cent of students study English and 68 per cent are studying Maths. How many students are studying both?

Solution

Given that the total number of students = 150
Students who study in English = 62% of 150 = {tex}\frac{62}{100} \times{/tex} 150 = 93
Students who study Maths = 68% of 150 = {tex}\frac{68}{100} \times{/tex} 150 = 102
Thus, Total students studying English and Maths = 93 + 102 = 195
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Students who study both English and maths = 195 - 150 = 45

  1. Total coloured shape = {tex}\frac{1}{8}+\frac{1}{8}+\frac{1}{8}+\frac{1}{16}+{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{16}+\frac{1}{4}+\frac{1}{4}{/tex} = 1
    1. Red coloured shape = {tex}\frac{1}{8}+\frac{1}{8}+\frac{1}{8}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1+1+1}{8}=\frac{3}{8}{/tex} [by observation]
      Hence, percentage of red coloured = {tex}\frac{\text { Red coloured shape }}{\text { Total coloured shape }} \times{/tex} 100% = {tex}\frac{3 / 8}{1} \times{/tex} 100% = {tex}\frac{3}{8} \times{/tex} 100% = 37.5%
    2. Blue coloured shape = {tex}\frac{1}{4}+\frac{1}{4}=\frac{1+1}{4}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2}{4}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} [by observation]
      Hence, percentage of blue coloured shape = {tex}\frac{\text { Blue coloured shape }}{\text { Total coloured shape }} \times{/tex} 100% = {tex}\frac{1 / 2}{1} \times{/tex} 100% = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \times{/tex} 100% = 50%
    3. Green coloured shape = {tex}\frac{1}{16}+\frac{1}{16}=\frac{1+1}{16}={/tex} {tex}\frac{2}{16}=\frac{1}{8}{/tex} [by observation]
      Hence, percentage of green coloured shape = {tex}\frac{\text { Green coloured shape }}{\text { Total coloured shape }} \times{/tex} 100% = {tex}\frac{1 / 8}{1} \times{/tex} 100% = {tex}\frac{1}{8} \times{/tex} 100% = 12.5%
  2. Sum of all Percentage calculated
    = Percentage of red coloured + percentage of blue coloured + percentage of green coloured
    = 37.5 + 50 + 12.5 [already calculated]
    = 100%
  3. If we rearrange the same pieces to form some other shape, the percentage of colours will not change, because we just rearrange the parts and not changing the percentage of colours.

Q 10

List three rational numbers between {tex}\frac{4}{5}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{5}{6}{/tex}

Solution

We convert the rational numbers {tex}\frac{4}{5}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{5}{6}{/tex} into rational numbers with the same denominators.
{tex}\frac{4}{5}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{4}{5}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{6}{6}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{24}{30}{/tex};
{tex}\frac{5}{6}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{5}{6}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{5}{5}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{25}{30}{/tex}
So, {tex}\frac{24}{30}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{24}{30}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{4}{4}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{96}{120}{/tex}
and {tex}\frac{25}{30}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{25}{30}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{4}{4}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{100}{120}{/tex}
Here, {tex}\frac{96}{120}{/tex} < {tex}\frac{97}{120}{/tex} < {tex}\frac{98}{120}{/tex} < {tex}\frac{99}{120}{/tex} < {tex}\frac{100}{120}{/tex}. So, the required numbers are {tex}\frac{97}{120}{/tex}, {tex}\frac{98}{120}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{99}{120}{/tex}
Alternate solution:
 A rational number between {tex}\frac{4}{5}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{5}{6}{/tex} is
= {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\left(\frac{4}{5}+\frac{5}{6}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{49}{60}{/tex}
another rational number
= {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\left(\frac{4}{5}+\frac{49}{60}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{97}{120}{/tex}
one more rational number
= {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\left(\frac{49}{60}+\frac{5}{6}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{99}{120}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{33}{40}{/tex}
Therefore, three rational numbers between {tex}\frac{4}{5}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{5}{6}{/tex} are {tex}\frac{49}{60}{/tex}, {tex}\frac{97}{120}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{33}{40}{/tex}
There can be many set of answers.

Q 11

Does the given pair represent equivalent rational numbers?
{tex}\frac{7}{12}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{28}{48}{/tex}

Solution

{tex}\frac{7}{12}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{28}{48}{/tex}
Now, first rational number is {tex}\frac{7}{12}{/tex} and it is already in the standard form because there is no common factor in 7 and 12 other than 1.
So, {tex}\frac{7}{12}{/tex} is in its standard form ...........(a)
Now, Consider {tex}\frac{28}{48}{/tex}
28 = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 7
48 = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 3
HCF = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 = 4
Now, to reduce the rational numbers to its standard form, we divide the numerator and denominator by their HCF. First, we take HCF of 28 and 48:
Now, {tex}\frac{28}{48}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{28 \div 4}{48 \div 4}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{7}{12}{/tex} ..........(b)
From (a) and (b), we can say that the rational numbers {tex}\frac{7}{12}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{28}{48}{/tex} are equivalent.

Q 12

Does the given pair represent equivalent rational numbers?
{tex}\frac{-2}{-3}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{-16}{24}{/tex}

Solution

{tex}\frac{-2}{-3}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{-16}{24}{/tex}
First we multiply the numerator and denominator of {tex}\frac{-2}{-3}{/tex} by (-1), we get
{tex}\frac{-2}{-3}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{(-2) \times(-1)}{(-3) \times(-1)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2}{3}{/tex} ............(a)
Now it is in its standard form.
Now, Consider {tex}\frac{16}{24}{/tex}
HCF of 16 and 24 is 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 = 8
16 = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2
24 = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 3
HCF = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 = 8
So, {tex}\frac{-16}{24}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-16 \div 8}{24 \div 8}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-2}{3}{/tex} ...........(b)
From (a) and (b), we can say that the rational numbers {tex}\frac{-2}{3}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{-16}{24}{/tex} are not equivalent.

Q 13

Write four more rational numbers to complete the pattern:
{tex}\frac{-1}{3}{/tex}, {tex}\frac{-2}{6}{/tex}, {tex}\frac{-3}{9}{/tex}, ________, ________, ________, ________.

Solution

By observing the above pattern, we find that denominator is multiple of 3. So we will increase this pattern in this way.
{tex}\frac{-2}{6}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-1 \times 2}{3 \times 2}, \frac{-3}{9}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-1 \times 3}{3 \times 3}, \frac{-4}{12}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-1 \times 4}{3 \times 4}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{-1 \times 1}{3 \times 1}=\frac{-1}{3},{/tex}
{tex}\frac{-1 \times 4}{3 \times 4}=\frac{-4}{12}{/tex}
Thus, we observe a pattern in these numbers.
So, the other numbers would be
{tex}\frac{-1 \times 5}{3 \times 5}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-5}{15}, \frac{-1 \times 6}{3 \times 6}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-6}{18}, \frac{-1 \times 7}{3 \times 7}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-7}{21}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{-1 \times 8}{3 \times 8}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-8}{24}{/tex}

Q 14

Find the sum of -4{tex}\frac{5}{6}{/tex} and -7{tex}\frac{3}{4}{/tex}

Solution

Here, -4{tex}\frac56{/tex} + {tex}\left(-7 \frac{3}{4}\right){/tex}
= {tex}\frac{-29}{6}{/tex} + {tex}\left(\frac{-31}{4}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{-29}{6}{/tex} + {tex}\frac{-31}{4}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{-29 \times 2}{12}{/tex} + {tex}\frac{-31 \times 3}{12}{/tex}   [Since LCM of 6 and 4 is 12].
= {tex}\frac{-29 \times 2-31 \times 3}{12}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{-58 -93}{12}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{-151}{12}{/tex}
So, the required sum is {tex}\frac{-151}{12}{/tex}

Q 15

Find the product of -2{tex}\frac34{/tex} and 5{tex}\frac67{/tex}

Solution

Here, {tex}-2 \frac{3}{4} \times 5 \frac{6}{7}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-11}{4} \times \frac{41}{7}{/tex}
Now, the product of two rational numbers
= {tex}\frac{\text { Product of numerators }}{\text { Product of denominators }}{/tex}
So, {tex}-2 \frac{3}{4} \times 5 \frac{6}{7}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-11}{4} \times \frac{41}{7}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-11 \times 41}{4 \times 7}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-451}{28}{/tex}

Q 16

Match column I to column II in the following:

Column I Column II
(a) {tex}\frac{3}{4} \div \frac{3}{4}{/tex} (i) -1
(b) {tex}\frac{1}{2} \div \frac{4}{3}{/tex} (ii) {tex}\frac{-2}{3}{/tex}
(c) {tex}\frac{2}{3} \div ({-1}){/tex} (iii) {tex}\frac{3}{2}{/tex}
(d) {tex}\frac{3}{4} \div \frac{1}{2}{/tex} (iv) {tex}\frac{3}{8}{/tex}
(e) {tex}\frac{5}{7} \div\left(\frac{-5}{7}\right){/tex} (v) 1
Solution

(a) - (v), (b) - (iv), (c) - (ii), (d) - (iii), (e) - (i).

Q 17

Find the reciprocal of {tex}\frac{2}{11} \div-\frac{5}{55}{/tex}

Solution

Given expression = {tex}\frac{2}{11} \div \frac{-5}{55}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2}{11} \times -\frac{55}{5}{/tex} = - 2
Now find out the reciprocal of -2
The reciprocal of - 2 is - {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}

Q 66

Match the following:

Column I Column II
(a) {tex}\frac {a}{b} \div \frac{a}{b}{/tex} (i) {tex}\frac {-a}{b}{/tex}
(b) {tex}\frac {a}{b} \div \frac {c}{d}{/tex} (ii) -1
(c) {tex}\frac {a}{b} \div \left({-1} \right){/tex} (iii) 1
(d) {tex}\frac {a}{b} \div \frac {-a}{b}{/tex} (iv) {tex}\frac {bc}{ad}{/tex}
(e) {tex}\frac{b}{a} \div\left(\frac{d}{c}\right){/tex} (v) {tex}\frac {ad}{bc}{/tex}
Solution

(a) - (iii), (b) - (v), (c) - (i), (d) - (ii), (e) - (iv).

  1. {tex}\frac {a}{b} \div \frac {a}{b}{/tex} = {tex}\frac {a}{b} \times \frac {b}{a}{/tex} = 1
  2. {tex}\frac {a}{b} \div \frac {c}{d}{/tex} = {tex}\frac {a}{b} \times \frac {d}{c}{/tex} = {tex}\frac {ad}{bc}{/tex}
  3. {tex}\frac{a}{b} \div(-1){/tex} = {tex}\frac{a}{b} \times(-1){/tex} = {tex}\frac {-a}{b}{/tex}
  4. {tex}\frac {a}{b} \div \frac {-a}{b}{/tex} = {tex}\frac {a}{b} \times \left (\frac {-b}{a} \right){/tex} = -1
  5. {tex}\frac {b}{a} \div \left (\frac {d}{c} \right){/tex} = {tex}\frac {b}{a} \times \frac {c}{d}{/tex} = {tex}\frac {bc}{ad}{/tex}
Q 67

Write each of the following rational numbers with positive denominators: {tex}\frac {5}{-8}{/tex}, {tex}\frac {15}{-28}{/tex}, {tex}\frac {-17}{-13}{/tex}

Solution

Rational numbers with positive denominators
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac {5}{-8}{/tex} = {tex}\frac {5 \times -1}{-8 \times -1}{/tex} = {tex}\frac {-5}{8}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac {15}{-28}{/tex} = {tex}\frac {15 \times -1}{-28 \times -1}{/tex} = {tex}\frac {-15}{28}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac {-17}{-13}{/tex} = {tex}\frac {-17 \times -1}{-13 \times -1}{/tex} = {tex}\frac {17}{13}{/tex}

Q 68

Express {tex}\frac {3}{4}{/tex} as a rational number with denominator 36.

Solution

To make the denominator 36, we multiply numerator and denominator by 9.
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac {3}{4}{/tex} = {tex}\frac {3 \times 9}{4 \times 9}{/tex} = {tex}\frac {27}{36}{/tex}

Q 69

Express {tex}\frac {3}{4}{/tex} as a rational number with denominator -80.

Solution

To make the denominator -80, multiply numerator and denominator of {tex}\frac {3}{4}{/tex} by -20.
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac {3}{4}{/tex} = {tex}\frac {3 \times (-20)}{4 \times (-20)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac {-60}{-80}{/tex}

Q 70

Reduce {tex}\frac {-60}{72}{/tex} rational number in its lowest form.

Solution

It can be written as,
{tex}\frac {-60}{72}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-60 \times \frac{1}{12}}{72 \times \frac{1}{12}}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac {-5}{6}{/tex}

Q 71

Reduce {tex}\frac {91}{-364}{/tex} rational number in its lowest form.

Solution

It can be written as
 {tex}\frac {91}{-364}{/tex} = {tex}\frac {91 \div 91}{-364 \div 91}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{91 \times \frac{1}{91}}{-364 \times \frac{1}{91}}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac {-1}{4}{/tex}

Q 72

Express {tex}\frac {-12}{-30}{/tex} rational number in its standard form.

Solution

Here,
{tex}\frac {-12}{-30}{/tex} = {tex}\frac {-12 \div 6}{-30 \div 6}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac {-2}{-5}{/tex} = {tex}\frac {2}{5}{/tex}
Therefore, the standard form of the given number is {tex}\frac {2}{5}{/tex}

Q 73

Express {tex}\frac {14}{-49}{/tex} rational number in its standard form.

Solution

Here,
{tex}\frac {14}{-49}{/tex} = {tex}\frac {14 \div 7}{-49 \div 7}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac {2}{-7}{/tex} 
Therefore, the standard form of given number is {tex}\frac {-2}{7}{/tex}

Q 74

Express {tex}\frac {-15}{35}{/tex} rational number in its standard form.

Solution

The standard form of a rational number
{tex}\frac {-15}{35}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac {-15 \div 5}{35 \div 5}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac {-3}{7}{/tex}
Therefore, the standard form of giving number is {tex}\frac {-3}{7}{/tex}

Q 75

Express {tex}\frac {299}{-161}{/tex} rational number in its standard form.

Solution

Standard form of rational number
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac {299}{-161}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac {299 \div 23}{-161 \div 23}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac {13}{-7}{/tex} = {tex}\frac {-13}{7}{/tex}
Therefore, the standard form of giving number is {tex}\frac {-13}{7}{/tex}

Q 76

Are the rational numbers {tex}\frac {-8}{28}{/tex} and {tex}\frac {32}{-112}{/tex} equivalent? Give reason.

Solution

Standard form of rational number
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac {-8}{28}{/tex} = {tex}\frac {-8 \div 4}{28 \div 4}{/tex} = {tex}\frac {-2}{7}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac {32}{-112}{/tex} = {tex}\frac {32 \div 16}{-112 \div 16}{/tex} = {tex}\frac {-2}{7}{/tex}
Therefore, the standard form of given number is {tex}\frac {-2}{7}{/tex}

Q 77

Arrange the rational numbers {tex}\frac {-7}{10}{/tex}, {tex}\frac {5}{8}{/tex}, {tex}\frac {2}{-3}{/tex}, {tex}\frac {-1}{4}{/tex}, {tex}\frac {3}{5}{/tex} in ascending order.

Solution

For rearranging the number in ascending order first we have to make denominator same of all given rational numbers.
Hence, LCM of 10, -8, -3, 4, and 5 is 120.
Now,
= {tex}\frac {-7 \times 12}{10 \times 12}{/tex}, {tex}\frac {5 \times 15}{-8 \times 15}{/tex}, {tex}\frac {2 \times 40}{-3 \times 40}{/tex}, {tex}\frac {-1 \times 30}{4 \times 30}{/tex}, {tex}\frac {-3 \times 24}{5 \times 24}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac {-84}{120}{/tex}, {tex}\frac {75}{-120}{/tex}, {tex}\frac {80}{-120}{/tex}, {tex}\frac {-30}{120}{/tex}, {tex}\frac {-72}{120}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac {-84}{120}{/tex}, {tex}\frac {-75}{120}{/tex}, {tex}\frac {-80}{120}{/tex}, {tex}\frac {-30}{120}{/tex}, {tex}\frac {-72}{120}{/tex}
Now, we arranged the number according to the value of the numerator,
So, {tex}\frac {-84}{120}{/tex} < {tex}\frac {-80}{120}{/tex} < {tex}\frac {-75}{120}{/tex} < {tex}\frac {-72}{120}{/tex} < {tex}\frac {-30}{120}{/tex}

Q 79

If {tex}\frac {-5}{7}{/tex} = {tex}\frac {x}{28}{/tex}, find the value of x.

Solution

According to the question,
{tex}\frac {-5}{7}{/tex} = {tex}\frac {x}{28}{/tex}
Using cross multiplication we get,
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} - 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 28 = 7 {tex}\times{/tex} x
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 7 {tex}\times{/tex} x = - 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 28
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex}\frac {-5 \times 28}{7}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = - 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 4
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = - 20
Hence, the value of x = - 20

Q 80

Give three rational numbers equivalent to {tex}\frac {-3}{4}{/tex}

Solution

If the numerator and denominator of a rational number is multiplied or divided by a non – zero integer, we get a rational number which is said to be equivalent of a given number.
First number equivalent to {tex}\frac {-3}{4}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac {-3 \times 2}{4 \times 2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac {-6}{8}{/tex}
Second number equivalent to {tex}\frac {-3}{4}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac {-3 \times 3}{4 \times 3}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac {-9}{12}{/tex}
Third number equivalent to {tex}\frac {-3}{4}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac {-3 \times 4}{4 \times 4}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac {-12}{16}{/tex}

Q 81

Give three rational numbers equivalent to {tex}\frac {7}{11}{/tex}

Solution

If the numerator and denominator of a rational number is multiplied or divided by a non – zero integer, we get a rational number which is said to be equivalent of a given number.
First number equivalent to {tex}\frac {7}{11}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac {7 \times 2}{11 \times 2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac {14}{22}{/tex}
Second number equivalent to {tex}\frac {7}{11}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac {7 \times 3}{11 \times 3}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac {21}{33}{/tex}
Third number equivalent to {tex}\frac {7}{11}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac {7 \times 4}{11 \times 4}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac {28}{44}{/tex}

Q 82

Write the next three rational numbers to complete the pattern.
{tex}\frac {4}{-5}{/tex}, {tex}\frac {8}{-10}{/tex}, {tex}\frac {12}{-15}{/tex}, {tex}\frac {16}{-20}{/tex}, ________, ________, ________.

Solution

Next three equivalent rational numbers are
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac {4}{-5}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac {4}{-5}{/tex} = {tex}\frac {4 \times 5}{-5 \times 5}{/tex} = {tex}\frac {20}{-25}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac {4}{-5}{/tex} = {tex}\frac {4 \times 6}{-5 \times 6}{/tex} = {tex}\frac {24}{-30}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac {4}{-5}{/tex} = {tex}\frac {4 \times 7}{-5 \times 7}{/tex} = {tex}\frac {28}{-35}{/tex}
Therefore, next equivalent rational numbers of a given number are {tex}\frac {20}{-25}{/tex}, {tex}\frac {24}{-30}{/tex} and {tex}\frac {28}{-35}{/tex}

Q 83

Write the next three rational numbers to complete the pattern.
{tex}\frac {-8}{7}{/tex}, {tex}\frac {-16}{14}{/tex}, {tex}\frac {-24}{21}{/tex}, {tex}\frac {-32}{28}{/tex}, ________, ________, ________.

Next three equivalent rational numbers are
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac {-8}{7}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac {-8}{7}{/tex} = {tex}\frac {-8 \times 5}{7 \times 5}{/tex} = {tex}\frac {-40}{35}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac {-8}{7}{/tex} = {tex}\frac {-8 \times 6}{7 \times 6}{/tex} = {tex}\frac {-48}{42}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac {-8}{7}{/tex} = {tex}\frac {-8 \times 7}{7 \times 7}{/tex} = {tex}\frac {-56}{49}{/tex}
Therefore, next equivalent rational numbers of a given number are {tex}\frac {-40}{35}{/tex}, {tex}\frac {-48}{42}{/tex} and {tex}\frac {-56}{49}{/tex}

Q 84

Q 86

Here, {tex}\frac {7}{3}{/tex} + {tex}\left (\frac {-4}{3} \right){/tex}

= {tex}\frac {7 - 4}{3}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac {3}{3}{/tex} = 1

Q 89

Q 91

Q 92

Q 95

Which is greater in the following?
-3{tex}\frac {5}{7}{/tex}, 3{tex}\frac {1}{9}{/tex}

Solution

Here, make the denominator same of both rational numbers
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}-3 \frac {5}{7}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{(-3 \ \times \ 7)\ + \ 5}{7}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-21\ + \ 5}{7}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-26}{7}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}-3 \frac {1}{9}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{(-3\ \times \ 9)\ +\ 1}{9}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{28}{9}{/tex}
Here, make the denominator same of both rational numbers
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{-26 \ \times \ 9}{7\ \times\ 9}{/tex} = - {tex}\frac{-234}{63}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{28 \ \times \ 7}{9\ \times\ 7}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{196}{63}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}-3 \frac {5}{7}{/tex} < {tex}3 \frac {1}{9}{/tex}
Therefore, the greater number is {tex}3 \frac {1}{9}{/tex}

Q 97

If x = {tex}\frac {1}{10}{/tex} and y = {tex}\frac {-3}{8}{/tex}, then evaluate x + y, x - y, x {tex}\times{/tex} y and x {tex}\div{/tex} y.

Solution

Given, x = {tex}\frac {1}{10}{/tex} and y = {tex}\frac {-3}{8}{/tex}
Now,
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x + y
= {tex}\frac {1}{10}{/tex} + {tex}\frac {-3}{8}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac {4 - 15}{40}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac {-11}{40}{/tex}
For,
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x - y
= {tex}\frac {1}{10}{/tex} - {tex}\frac {-3}{8}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac {4 + 15}{40}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac {19}{40}{/tex}
Now,
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x {tex}\times{/tex} y
= {tex}\frac {1}{10}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac {-3}{8}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac {1 \times (-3)}{10 \times 8}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac {-3}{80}{/tex}
Also,
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x {tex}\times{/tex} y
= {tex}\frac {1}{10}{/tex} {tex}\div{/tex} {tex}\frac {-3}{8}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac {1}{10}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac {8}{-3}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac {1 \times 8}{10 \times (-3)}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac {8}{-30}{/tex} = {tex}\frac {-4}{15}{/tex}

Q 98

Find the reciprocal of the {tex}\left (\frac {1}{2} \times \frac {1}{4} \right){/tex} + {tex}\left (\frac {1}{2} \times {6} \right){/tex}

= {tex}\frac {25}{8}{/tex}
Therefore, the reciprocal is {tex}\frac {8}{25}{/tex}

Q 101

Q 105

Given that {tex}\frac {p}{q}{/tex} and {tex}\frac {r}{s}{/tex} are two rational numbers with different denominators and both of them are in standard form. To compare these rational numbers we say that:

  1. {tex}\frac {\square}{\square}{/tex} < {tex}\frac {\square}{\square}{/tex}, if p {tex}\times{/tex} s < r {tex}\times{/tex} q
  2. {tex}\frac {p}{q}{/tex} = {tex}\frac {r}{s}{/tex}, if ________ = ________
  3. {tex}\frac {\square}{\square}{/tex} > {tex}\frac {\square}{\square}{/tex}, if p {tex}\times{/tex} s > r {tex}\times{/tex} q
Solution
  1. if p {tex}\times{/tex} s < r {tex}\times{/tex} q then,
    By transferring sides.....
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac {p}{q}{/tex} < {tex}\frac {r}{s}{/tex}
  2. If {tex}\frac {p}{q}{/tex} = {tex}\frac {r}{s}{/tex}, then,
    By cross-multiplication
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} p {tex}\times{/tex} s = r {tex}\times{/tex} q
  3. If p {tex}\times{/tex} s > r {tex}\times{/tex} q
    By transferring sides....
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac {p}{q}{/tex} > {tex}\frac {r}{s}{/tex}

Q 110

Write the rational number in its standard form: 35%

Solution

By using prime factorization, we get,
35% = {tex}\frac {35}{100}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 35 = 7 {tex}\times{/tex} 5
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 100 = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 5
So, HCF is 5
Now,
{tex}\frac {35 \div 5}{100 \div 5}{/tex} = {tex}\frac {7}{20}{/tex}
