Q 115

Find a rational number exactly halfway between {tex}\frac {-1}{3}{/tex} and {tex}\frac {1}{3}{/tex}.

Solution

As we know that exactly halfway between two rational numbers is = {tex}\frac {a + b}{2}{/tex}
Rational numbers are {tex}\frac {-1}{3}{/tex} and {tex}\frac {1}{3}{/tex}
Here, a = {tex}\frac {-1}{3}{/tex} and b = {tex}\frac {1}{3}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac {a + b}{2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\frac{-1}{3}+\frac{1}{3}}{2}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac {0}{2}{/tex}
= 0
Therefore, the exact halfway between {tex}\frac {-1}{3}{/tex} and {tex}\frac {1}{3}{/tex} is zero.

Q 116

Find a rational number exactly halfway between {tex}\frac {1}{6}{/tex} and {tex}\frac {1}{9}{/tex}.

Solution

As we know that exactly halfway between two rational numbers is = {tex}\frac {a \ +\ b}{2}{/tex}
Rational numbers are {tex}\frac {1}{6}{/tex} and {tex}\frac {1}{9}{/tex}
Here, a = {tex}\frac {1}{6}{/tex} and b = {tex}\frac {1}{9}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac {a\ +\ b}{2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\frac{1}{6}\ +\ \frac{1}{9}}{2}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{\frac{3 \ + \ 2}{18}}{2}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac {5}{18\ \times\ 2}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac {5}{36}{/tex}
Therefore, the exact halfway between {tex}\frac {1}{6}{/tex} and {tex}\frac {1}{9}{/tex} is {tex}\frac {5}{36}{/tex}.

Q 117

Find a rational number exactly halfway between {tex}\frac {5}{-13}{/tex} and {tex}\frac {-7}{9}{/tex}.

Solution

As we know that exactly halfway between two rational numbers is = {tex}\frac {a \ +\ b}{2}{/tex}
Rational numbers are {tex}\frac {5}{-13}{/tex} and {tex}\frac {-7}{9}{/tex}
Here, a = {tex}\frac {5}{-13}{/tex} and b = {tex}\frac {-7}{9}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac {a \ +\ b}{2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\frac{5}{-13}\ +\ \frac{-7}{9}}{2}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{\frac{-45 \ -\ 91}{117}}{2}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac {-136}{117\ \times\ 2}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac {-136}{234}{/tex}
Therefore, the exact halfway between {tex}\frac {5}{-13}{/tex} and {tex}\frac {-7}{9}{/tex} is {tex}\frac {-136}{234}{/tex}.

Q 118

Find a rational number exactly halfway between {tex}\frac {1}{15}{/tex} and {tex}\frac {1}{12}{/tex}.

Solution

As we know that exactly halfway between two rational numbers is = {tex}\frac {a \ +\ b}{2}{/tex}
Rational numbers are {tex}\frac {1}{15}{/tex} and {tex}\frac {1}{12}{/tex}
Here, a = {tex}\frac {1}{15}{/tex} and b = {tex}\frac {1}{12}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac {a\ +\ b}{2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\frac{1}{15}\ +\ \frac{1}{12}}{2}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{\frac{4 \ +\ 5}{60}}{2}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac {9}{60\ \times\ 2}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac {9}{120}{/tex} = {tex}\frac {3}{40}{/tex}
Therefore, the exact halfway between {tex}\frac {1}{15}{/tex} and {tex}\frac {1}{12}{/tex} is {tex}\frac {3}{40}{/tex}.

Q 119

Taking x = {tex}\frac {-4}{9}{/tex}, y = {tex}\frac {5}{12}{/tex} and z = {tex}\frac {7}{18}{/tex}, find:

  1. the rational number which when added to x gives y.
  2. the rational number which subtracted from y gives z.
  3. the rational number which when added to z gives us x.
  4. the rational number which when multiplied by y to get x.
  5. the reciprocal of x + y.
  6. the sum of reciprocals of x and y.
  7. (x {tex}\div{/tex} y) {tex}\times{/tex} z
  8. (x - y) + z
  9. x + (y + z)
  10. x {tex}\div{/tex} (y {tex}\div{/tex} z)
  11. x - (y + z)
Solution

Given, x = {tex}\frac {-4}{9}{/tex}, y = {tex}\frac {5}{12}{/tex} and z = {tex}\frac {7}{18}{/tex}

  1. We add A to x to get y.
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} A + x = y
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} A + {tex}\frac {-4}{9}{/tex} = {tex}\frac {5}{12}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} A = {tex}\frac {5}{12}{/tex} + {tex}\frac {4}{9}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} A = {tex}\frac {15 + 16}{36}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} A = {tex}\frac {31}{36}{/tex}
  2. We Subtract A to y to get z.
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y - A = z
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac {5}{12}{/tex} - A = {tex}\frac {7}{18}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} A = {tex}\frac {7}{18}{/tex} - {tex}\frac {5}{12}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} A = {tex}\frac {14 + 15}{36}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} A = {tex}\frac {-1}{36}{/tex}
  3. We add A to z to get x.
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} A + z = x
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} A + {tex}\frac {7}{18}{/tex} = {tex}\frac {-4}{9}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} A = {tex}\frac {-4}{9}{/tex} - {tex}\frac {7}{18}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} A = {tex}\frac {-8 -7}{18}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} A = {tex}\frac {5}{6}{/tex}
  4. We Subtract A to y to get x.
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} A {tex}\times{/tex} z = x
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} A {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac {5}{12}{/tex} = {tex}\frac {-4}{9}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} A = {tex}\frac {-4}{9}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac {12}{5}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} A = {tex}\frac {-48}{45}{/tex}
  5. Here, x + y
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x + y = {tex}\frac {-4}{9}{/tex} + {tex}\frac {5}{12}{/tex}
    = {tex}\frac {-16 +15}{36}{/tex}
    = {tex}\frac {-1}{36}{/tex}
    Therefore, reciprocal of x + y = {tex}\frac {1}{-1 \div 36}{/tex} = - 36
  6. Reciprocal of x and y is {tex}\frac {1}{x}{/tex} and {tex}\frac {1}{y}{/tex}
    sum of reciprocals = {tex}\frac {1}{x}{/tex} + {tex}\frac {1}{y}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac {1}{x}{/tex} + {tex}\frac {1}{y}{/tex}
    = {tex}\frac {-9}{4}{/tex} + {tex}\frac {12}{5}{/tex}
    = {tex}\frac {-45 +48}{20}{/tex}
    = {tex}\frac{3}{20}{/tex}
  7. Given, (x {tex}\div{/tex} y) {tex}\times{/tex} z
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x {tex}\div{/tex} y) {tex}\times{/tex} z
    = {tex}\left(\frac{-4}{9} \div \frac{5}{12}\right) \times \frac{7}{18}{/tex}
    = {tex}\frac{-4 \times 12 \times 7}{9 \times 5 \times 18}{/tex}
    = {tex}\frac{-56}{135}{/tex}
  8. Given, (x - y) + z
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x - y) + z
    = {tex}\left(\frac{-4}{9}-\frac{5}{12}\right)+\frac{7}{18}{/tex}
    = {tex}\left(\frac{-16-15}{36}\right)+\frac{7}{18}{/tex}
    = {tex}\frac{-31}{36}+\frac{7}{18}{/tex}
    = {tex}\frac{-31+14}{36}{/tex}
    = {tex}\frac{-17}{36}{/tex}
  9. We have x + (y + z)
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x + (y + z)
    = {tex}\frac{-4}{9}+\left(\frac{5}{12}+\frac{7}{18}\right){/tex}
    = {tex}\frac{-4}{9}+\frac{15+14}{36}{/tex}
    = {tex}\frac{-4 \times 4+29}{36}{/tex}
    = {tex}\frac{13}{36}{/tex}
  10. Given, x {tex}\div{/tex} (y {tex}\div{/tex} z)
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x {tex}\div{/tex} (y {tex}\div{/tex} z)
    = {tex}\frac{-4}{9} \div\left(\frac{5}{12} \div \frac{7}{18}\right){/tex}
    = {tex}\frac{-4 \times 12 \times 7}{9 \times 5 \times 18}{/tex}
    = {tex}\frac{-336}{810}{/tex}
    = {tex}\frac{-56}{135}{/tex}
  11. Given, x - (y + z)
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x - (y + z)
    = {tex}\frac{-4}{9}-\left(\frac{5}{12} \div \frac{7}{18}\right){/tex}
    = {tex}\frac{-4}{9}-\left(\frac{15+14}{36}\right){/tex}
    = {tex}\frac{-16-29}{36}{/tex}
    = {tex}\frac{-45}{36}{/tex}
    = {tex}\frac{-5}{4}{/tex}
Q 120

What should be added to {tex}\frac {-1}{2}{/tex} to obtain the nearest natural number?

Solution

As we know that the nearest number of {tex}\frac {-1}{2}{/tex} is 1.
Let y be added {tex}\frac {-1}{2}{/tex} to obtain 1.
Then,
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac {-1}{2}{/tex} + y = 1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = 1 + {tex}\frac {1}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = {tex}\frac{2+1}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = {tex}\frac {3}{2}{/tex}
Therefore, {tex}\frac {3}{2}{/tex} should be added be added {tex}\frac {-1}{2}{/tex} to obtain the nearest natural number.

Q 121

What should be subtracted from {tex}\frac{-2}{3}{/tex} to obtain the nearest integer?

Solution

As we know that the nearest number of {tex}\frac {-2}{3}{/tex} is - 1.
Let y be added {tex}\frac {-2}{3}{/tex} to obtain - 1.
Then,
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac {-2}{3}{/tex} - y = - 1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = {tex}\frac {-2}{3}{/tex} + 1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = {tex}\frac{-2+3}{3}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = {tex}\frac {1}{3}{/tex}
Therefore, {tex}\frac {1}{3}{/tex} should be added be added {tex}\frac {-2}{3}{/tex} to obtain the nearest natural number.

Q 122

What should be multiplied with {tex}\frac{-5}{8}{/tex} to obtain the nearest integer?

Solution

As we know that nearest number of {tex}\frac{-5}{8}{/tex} is - 1.
Let y be the number.
ACQ,
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{-5}{8}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} y = - 1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = - 1 {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{-5}{8}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = {tex}\frac{8}{5}{/tex}
Therefore, {tex}\frac{8}{5}{/tex} is the required number.

Q 123

What should be divided to {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} to obtain the greatest negative integer?

Solution

As we know that greatest negative number is - 1.
Let y be the number.
ACQ,
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\div {/tex} y = - 1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{y}{/tex} = - 1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = {tex}\frac{-1}{2}{/tex}
Therefore, {tex}\frac{-1}{2}{/tex} is the required number.

Q 124

From a rope 68 m long, pieces of equal size are cut. If length of one piece is 4{tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex} m, find the number of such pieces.

Solution

Given, length of the rope is 68 meters.
Length of small pieces = 4{tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex} m = {tex}\frac{17}{4}{/tex} m
Hence, number of pieces = {tex}\frac{\frac{68}{17}}{4}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{68}{1} \times \frac{4}{17}{/tex}
= 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 4 = 16
Therefore, 16 is the number of pieces.

Q 125

If 12 shirts of equal size can be prepared from 27m cloth, what is the length of cloth required for each shirt?

Solution

Given, total size of available cloth is 27 meters
since, 12 shirts can be made from 27 meters long cloth.
Length of cloth required for each shirt
= {tex}\frac{\text { total available cloth }}{\text { Number of shirt }}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{27}{12}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{9}{4}{/tex}
= 2.25 m
Therefore, 2.25 meters of cloth is required for each shirt.

Q 126

Insert 3 equivalent rational numbers between: {tex}\frac{-1}{2}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{1}{5}{/tex}.

Solution

First rational number = {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{-1}{2}+\frac{1}{5}\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{-5\ +\ 2}{10} \right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{-3}{20}{/tex}
Second rational number = {tex}\frac{-3\ \times \ 2}{20\ \times\ 2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-6}{40}{/tex}
Third equivalent rational number = {tex}\frac{-3\ \times\ 3}{20\ \times\ 3}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-9}{60}{/tex}

Q 127

Insert 3 equivalent rational numbers between 0 and -10.

Solution

First rational number = {tex}\frac{1}{2}(0+(-10)){/tex}
= {tex}\frac{1}{2}(-10){/tex} = - 5
Second rational number = {tex}\frac{-10}{2}{/tex}
Third rational number = {tex}\frac{-15}{3}{/tex}

Q 128

Put the (√), wherever applicable

Number Natural Number Whole Number Integer Fraction Rational Number
(a) - 114          
(b) {tex}\frac{19}{27}{/tex}          
(c) {tex}\frac{623}{1}{/tex}          
(d) {tex}-19 \frac{3}{4}{/tex}          
(e) {tex}\frac{73}{71}{/tex}          
(f) 0          
Solution

As we know that,
Integers are -2, -1, 0, 1, 2, 3
Whole numbers are 0, 1, 2, 3
Natural numbers are 1, 2, 3, 4........
Rational numbers are {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}, {tex}\frac{3}{5}{/tex}, {tex}\frac{7}{2}{/tex}
Hence, according to the number systems,

  1.  - 114 integers are rational numbers
  2.  {tex}\frac{19}{27}{/tex} fraction and ration numbers
  3.  {tex}\frac{623}{1}{/tex} The whole number, integer, fraction, natural number and rational number
  4.  {tex}-19 \frac{3}{4}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-79}{4}{/tex} Rational number
  5.  {tex}\frac{73}{71}{/tex} Fraction and rational number
  6. 0 The whole number, integer, fraction, and rational number

Therefore the table is,

Number Natural Number Whole Number Integer Fraction Rational Number
(a) - 114     √   √
(b) {tex}\frac{19}{27}{/tex}       √ √
(c) {tex}\frac{623}{1}{/tex} √ √ √ √ √
(d) {tex}-19 \frac{3}{4}{/tex}         √
(e) {tex}\frac{73}{71}{/tex}       √ √
(f) 0   √ √ √ √
Q 129

a and b are two different numbers taken from the numbers 1 - 50. What is the largest value that {tex}\frac{a\ -\ b}{a\ +\ b}{/tex} can have? What is the largest value that {tex}\frac{a\ +\ b}{a\ -\ b}{/tex} can have?

Solution

For the largest value of {tex}\frac{a\ -\ b}{a\ +\ b}{/tex} .
Let’s a = 50 and b = 1;
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{a\ -\ b}{a\ +\ b}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{50-1}{50+1}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{49}{51}{/tex}
For the largest value of {tex}\frac{a\ +\ b}{a\ -\ b}{/tex} .
Let’s a = 50 and b = 49;
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{a\ +\ b}{a\ -\ b}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{50\ +\ 49}{50\ -\ 49}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{99}{1}{/tex}

Q 130

150 students are studying English, Maths or both. 62 per cent of the students are studying English and 68 per cent are studying Maths. How many students are studying both?

Solution

Students studying English, Maths or both are = 150
Student studying English are = {tex}\frac{62 \ \times \ 150}{100}{/tex} = 93
Now, students studying Maths are = {tex}\frac{68\ \times\ 150}{100}{/tex} = 102
Using the concept of sets,
Students studying both = Students studying Maths + Students Studying English - Students studying both English, Maths or both
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Students studying both = 102 + 93 - 150
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Students studying both = 195 - 150
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Students studying both = 45

Q 131

A body floats {tex}\frac{2}{9}{/tex} of its volume above the surface. What is the ratio of the body submerged volume to its exposed volume? Re-write it as a rational number.

Solution

Let’s volume of the body = V
So, Exposed volume = {tex}\frac{2}{9} V{/tex}
Now, the submerged volume of the body = V - {tex}\frac{2}{9} V{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{7}{9} V{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Ratio of submerged volume to it’s exposed volume
{tex}=\frac{\frac{7}{2} v}{\frac{2}{9}v}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{7}{2}{/tex} = 7:2
As a Rational number = {tex}\frac{7}{2}{/tex}

Q 132

Find the odd one out of the following and give a reason.

  1. {tex}\frac{4}{3} \times \frac{3}{4}{/tex}
  2. {tex}\frac{-3}{2} \times \frac{-2}{3}{/tex}
  3. {tex}2 \times \frac{1}{2}{/tex}
  4. {tex}\frac{-1}{3} \times \frac{3}{1}{/tex}
Solution
  1. Given,
    {tex}\frac{4}{3} \times \frac{3}{4}{/tex}
    = {tex}\frac{4 \times 3}{3 \times 4}=\frac{12}{12}{/tex} = 1
  2. Given,
    {tex}\frac{-3}{2} \times \frac{-2}{3}{/tex}
    = {tex}\frac{-3 \times-2}{2 \times 3}=\frac{6}{6}{/tex} = 1
  3. Given,
    {tex}2 \times \frac{1}{2}{/tex}
    = {tex}\frac{2 \times 1}{1 \times 2}=\frac{2}{2}{/tex}
    = 1
  4. Given,
    {tex}\frac{-1}{3} \times \frac{3}{1}{/tex}
    = {tex}\frac{-1 \times 3}{3 \times 1}=\frac{-3}{3}{/tex}
    = - 1
    D is the odd one.
Q 133

Find the odd one out of the following and give a reason.

  1. {tex}\frac{4}{-9}{/tex}
  2. {tex}\frac{-16}{36}{/tex}
  3. {tex}\frac{-20}{-45}{/tex}
  4. {tex}\frac{28}{-63}{/tex}
Q 134

Find the odd one out of the following and give a reason.

  1. {tex}\frac{-4}{3}{/tex}
  2. {tex}\frac{-7}{6}{/tex}
  3. {tex}\frac{-10}{3}{/tex}
  4. {tex}\frac{-8}{7}{/tex}
Q 135

Find the odd one out of the following and give a reason.

  1. {tex}\frac{-3}{7}{/tex}
  2. {tex}\frac{-9}{15}{/tex}
  3. {tex}\frac{+24}{20}{/tex}
  4. {tex}\frac{+35}{25}{/tex}
Q 136

What’s the Error? Chhaya simplified a rational number in this manner {tex}\frac{-25}{-30}=\frac{-5}{6}{/tex}. What error did the student make?

Solution

If a denominator and numerator both have a negative sign, then it will cancel out each other and the resulting fraction will be a positive rational number.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{-25}{-30}=\frac{-5}{6}{/tex}
Here, Chhaya divided numerator by 5 but denominator by -5.

Q 13

Construct a right-angled triangle whose hypotenuse measures 5 cm and one of the other sides measures 3.2 cm.

Solution

Steps of construction

  1. Draw a line AB = 3.2 cm.
  2. Construct a right angle (90o) at point B, i.e. {tex}\angle{/tex} ABY = 90o 
  3. Now, from point A, cut an arc 5 cm on BY at C.
  4. Joint C to A.

Hence, {tex}\Delta{/tex}ABC is the required triangle, having hypotenuse AC = 5 cm and AB = 3.2 cm.

Q 14

Construct a right-angled isosceles triangle with one side (other than hypotenuse) of length 4.5 cm.

Solution

Steps of construction

  1. Draw a line AB = 4.5 cm
  2. Construct a right angle (90o) at point B, i.e. {tex}\angle{/tex}ABY = 90o.
  3. From point B, cut an arc 4.5 cm on BY at C.
  4. Join C to A.

Hence, {tex}\Delta{/tex}ABC is the required triangle having AB - AC = 4.5 cm.

Q 15

Draw two parallel lines at a distance of 2.2 cm apart.

Solution

Steps of construction

  1. Draw a line I and mark a point C outside it.
  2. Take a point B on the line I and join BC.
  3. Draw line parallel to line I passing through C.
  4. Mark a point D on line m, at a distance of 2.2 cm from C.
  5. Through D draw AD || BC.

Line I is parallel to line m

Also, AD || BC, AB = DC = 2.2 cm.

Q 16

Draw an isosceles triangle with each of equal sides of length 3 cm and the angle between them as 45o.

Solution

Steps of construction

  1. Firstly, we draw a rough sketch of a triangle with given measures marked on it.
  2. Draw a line segment AB of length 3 cm.
  3. Draw an angle of 45o on point B and produce it to ray Y.
  4. With B as the centre; draw an arc of 3 cm which intersects ray BY at C.
  5. Join AC


Thus, {tex}\Delta{/tex}ABC is the required isosceles triangle.

Q 17

Draw a triangle whose sides are of lengths 4 cm, 5 cm and 7 cm.

Solution

Let us assume that given sides are BC = 7 cm, AB = 4 cm and AC = 5 cm 
Steps of construction

  1. Draw a line BC = 7 cm
  2. With center B and radius 4 cm draw an arc.
  3. With center C and radius 5 cm, draw an arc which cuts the previous arc at A.
  4. Join AB and AC.


Hence, {tex}\Delta{/tex}ABC is the required triangle in which AB = 4 cm, BC = 7 cm and AC = 5 cm.

Q 18

Construct an obtuse-angled triangle which has a base of 5.5 cm and base angles of 30o and 120o.

Solution

Steps of construction

  1. Draw a line segment BC of length 5.5 cm.
  2. Draw an angle of 120o on point B and produce it to ray Y.
  3. Draw an angle of 30o on point C and produce it to ray X.
  4. Extend BY and CX to intersect at point A.

Hence, {tex}\Delta{/tex}ABC is the required triangle with BC = 5.5, {tex}\angle{/tex}ABC = 120o and {tex}\angle{/tex}ACB = 30o.

Q 19

Construct an equilateral triangle ABC of side 6 cm.

Solution

Steps of construction

  1. Draw a line segment AB = 6 cm
  2. Draw an arc of radius 6 cm from point A.
  3. Now; draw another arc of radius 6 cm from point B to cut the previous arc at C.
  4. Join A to C and B to C.


Hence, {tex}\Delta{/tex}ABC is the required triangle.

Q 20

By what minimum angle does a regular hexagon rotate so as to coincide with its original position for the first time?

Solution

A regular hexagon must be rotated through a minimum angle = 60o
So, that it can coincide with its original position for the first time.
{tex}\because{/tex} Angle of rotation of hexagon = {tex}\frac{360^{\circ}}{ Number \ of \ Sides}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{360^{\circ}}{6}{/tex} = 60o

Q 10

A nursery school play ground is 160 m long and 80 m wide. In it 80 m {tex}\times{/tex} 80 m is kept for swings and in the remaining portion, there are 1.5 m wide path parallel to its width and parallel to its remaining length as shown in fig. The remaining area is covered by grass. Find the area covered by grass.

Solution

Area of school playground is 160 m {tex}\times{/tex} 80 m = 12800 m2
Area kept for swings = 80 m {tex}\times{/tex} 80 m = 6400 m2
Area of path parallel to the width of playground
= 80 m {tex}\times{/tex} 1.5 m = 120 m2
Area of path parallel to the remaining length of
playground
= 80 m {tex}\times{/tex} 1.5 m = 120 m2
Area common to both paths = 1.5 m {tex}\times{/tex} 1.5 m = 2.25 m2
[since it is taken twice for measurement it is to be subtracted from the area of paths]
Total area covered by both the paths
= (120 + 120 - 2.25) m2
= 237.75 m2
Area covered by grass = Area of school playground - (Area kept for swings + Area covered by paths)
= 12800 m2 - [ 6400 + 237.75] m2
= (12800 - 6637.75) m2
= 6162.25 m2

Q 11

In the figure, ABCD is a parallelogram, in which AB = 8 cm, AD = 6 cm and altitude AE = 4 cm. Find the altitude corresponding to side AD.

Solution

Area of parallelogram ABCD = AB {tex}\times{/tex} AE = 8 {tex}\times{/tex} 4 cm2
= 32 cm2
Let altitude corresponding to AD be h. Then,
h {tex}\times{/tex} AD = 32
or h {tex}\times{/tex} 6 = 32
or h = {tex}\frac{32}{6}=\frac{16}{3}{/tex} 
Thus, altitude corresponding to AD is {tex}\frac{16}{3}{/tex} cm.

Q 12

A rectangular shaped swimming pool with dimensions 30 m {tex}\times{/tex} 20 m has 5 m wide cemented path along its length and 8 m wide path along its width (as shown in figure). Find the cost of cementing the path at the rate of ₹200 per m2.

Solution

Area covered by swimming pool = 30 m {tex}\times{/tex} 20 m = 600 m2
Length of outer rectangle = (30 + 8 + 8) m = 46 m
and its breadth = (20 + 5 + 5) m = 30 m
So, the area of outer rectangle
= 46 m {tex}\times{/tex} 30 m = 1380 m2
Area of cemented path = Area of outer rectangle – Area of swimming pool
= (1380 - 600) m2 = 780 m2
Cost of cementing 1 m2 path = ₹200
So, total cost of cementing the path
= ₹780 {tex}\times{/tex} 200
= ₹156000

Q 13

The circumference of a circle is 33 cm. Find its area.

Solution

Let the radius of the circle be r.
Then, 2{tex}\pi{/tex}r = 33
i.e., r = {tex}\frac{33}{2 \pi}=\frac{33}{2} \times \frac{7}{22}=\frac{21}{4}{/tex} 
Thus, radius is {tex}\frac{21}{4}{/tex} cm
So, the area of the circle = {tex}\pi{/tex}r2 = {tex}\frac{22}{7} \cdot \frac{21}{4} \cdot \frac{21}{4}=\frac{693}{8}{/tex} 
Thus, the area of the circle is {tex}\frac{693}{8}{/tex} cm2

Q 14

Rectangle ABCD is formed in a circle as shown in the figure. If AE = 8 cm and AD = 5 cm, find the perimeter of the rectangle.

Solution

DE = EA + AD = (8 + 5)cm = 13 cm
DE is the radius of the circle.
Also, DB is the radius of the circle.
Next, AC = DB [Since diagonals of a rectangle are equal in length]
Therefore, AC = 13 cm.
From {tex}\triangle{/tex}ADC, DC2 = AC2 – AD2 = 132 – 52 = 169 – 25 = 144 = 122
So, DC = 12
Thus, the length of DC is 12 cm.
Hence, perimeter of the rectangle ABCD
= 2 (12 + 5)cm = 34 cm.

Q 15

Find the area of a parallelogram-shaped shaded region of the figure. Also, find the area of each triangle. What is the ratio of the area of shaded portion to the remaining area of the rectangle?

Solution

ABCD is a rectangle and DEBF is a parallelogram,
Since {tex}\triangle{/tex}DAF {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}BCE, therefore their areas will be equal.
Area of {tex}\triangle{/tex}DAF = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \times 4 \times 6{/tex} cm2 = 12 cm2
Area of rectangle = l {tex}\times{/tex} b = 10 cm {tex}\times{/tex} 6 cm = 60 cm2
Area of shaded region = Area of rectangle - Area of {tex}\triangle{/tex}DAF - Area of {tex}\triangle{/tex}BCE = (60 - 12 - 12)cm2
= (60 - 24)cm2 = 36 cm2
Area of remaining part = Area of Rectangle - Area of shaded portion = (60 - 36) cm2 = 24 cm2
Ratio = Area of shaded portion : Area of remaining rectangle
= 36 : 24 = 3 : 2

Q 70

A hedge boundary needs to be planted around a rectangular lawn of size 72 m {tex}\times{/tex} 18 m. If 3 shrubs can be planted in a metre of hedge, how many shrubs will be planted in all?

Solution

Here, length of rectangular lawn = 72 m and breadth of rectangular lawn = 18 m
{tex}\because{/tex} Perimeter of rectangle = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} (Length + Breadth)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Perimeter of rectangular lawn = 2 (72 + 18) = 2 (90) = 180 m
If 3 shrubs can be planted in a metre of hedge.
Then, number of shrubs = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} Perimeter of rectangular lawn = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 180 = 540

Q 71

People of Khejadli village take good care of plants, trees, and animals. They say that plants and animals can survive without us, but we can not survive without them. Inspired by her elders Amrita marked some land for her pets (camel and ox) and plants. Find the ratio of the areas kept for animals and plants to the living area.

Solution

We know that,
Area of rectangle = l {tex}\times{/tex} b and area of circle = {tex}\pi{/tex}r2
From the given figure,
Area of total rectangular land = 15 m {tex}\times{/tex} 10 m = 150 m2
Area of land covered by plants = 9 m {tex}\times{/tex} 1 m = 9 m2
Area of land covered by camel = 5 m {tex}\times{/tex} 3 m = 15 m2
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Region of land covered by ox is circular area.
So, diameter, d = 2.8 m
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Radius = {tex}\frac{d}{2}=\frac{2.8}{2}{/tex} = 1.4 m [{tex}\because{/tex} radius = {tex}\frac{\text { diameter }}{2}{/tex}]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Area of land covered by ox = {tex}\pi r^{2}=\frac{22}{7} \times 1.4 \times 1.4{/tex} = 6.16 m2 
Total area covered by plants, camel and ox = 9 + 15 + 6.16 = 30.16 m2 
Remaining land for living = Total area - Area covered by plants and animals 
= (150 - 30.16)m2 = 119.84 m2
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Ratio of areas kept for animals and plants to the living area
= 30.16 : 119.84 = 3016 : 11984 = 377 : 1498
The value depicted here is that we should save our environment and balance the environment.

Q 72

The perimeter of a rectangle is 40 m. Its length is four metres less than five times its breadth. Find the area of the rectangle.

Solution

Let breadth of the rectangle = x
Then, length of the rectangle = 5x - 4
We know that,
Perimeter of rectangle = 2(l + b)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 40 = 2(l + b)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 40 = 2(5x - 4 + x)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 40 = 2(6x - 4)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 12x - 8 = 40
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 12x = 40 + 8
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 12x = 48
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex}\frac{48}{12}{/tex} = 4
So, breadth = x = 4m and length = 5x - 4 = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 4 - 4 = 16 m
{tex}\because{/tex} Area of rectangle = l {tex}\times{/tex} b = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 16 = 64 m2 
Hence, the area of the rectangle is 64 m2 

Q 73

A wall of a room is of dimensions 5 m {tex}\times{/tex} 4 m. It has a window of dimensions 1.5 m {tex}\times{/tex} 1m and a door of dimensions 2.25 m {tex}\times{/tex} 1m. Find the area of the wall which is to be painted.

Solution

Given, a wall of a room is of dimensions 5 m {tex}\times{/tex} 4m.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Length of the room = 5 m and breadth of the room = 4 m
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Area of the room = l {tex}\times{/tex} b = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 4 = 20 m2
Also, length of the window = 1.5 m and breadth of the window = 1 m [given]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Area of the window = l {tex}\times{/tex} b = 1.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 = 1.5 m2
Now, length of the door = 2.25 m and breadth of the door = 1 m
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Area of the door = l {tex}\times{/tex} b = 2.25 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 = 2.25 m2
Now, area of the wall to be painted = Area of the room - (Area of the window + Area of the door)
= 20 - (1.5 + 2.25) = 20 - 3.75 = 16.25 m2

Q 74

Rectangle MNOP is made up of four congruent rectangles (in a given figure). If the area of one of the rectangles is 8 m2 and breadth is 2 m, then find the perimeter of MNOP.

Solution


We have, area of one rectangle = 8m2 and breadth = 2m 
We know that,
Area of rectangle = l {tex}\times{/tex} b
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} l {tex}\times{/tex} b = 8 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} l {tex}\times{/tex} 2 = 8 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} l = 4 m
Now, we have to find the perimeter of MNOP, which contains four congruent rectangles, it means they have same length and breadth.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Perimeter of rectangle MNOP = MN + NC + CD + DO + PO + PF + FA + MA
= 4 + 2 + 4 + 2 + 4 + 2 + 4 + 2
= 24 m
Hence, the perimeter of MNOP is 24 m.

Q 75

In a figure, area of {tex}\triangle{/tex}AFB is equal to the area of parallelogram ABCD. If altitude EF is 16 cm long, find the altitude of the parallelogram to the base AB of length 10 cm. What is the area of {tex}\triangle{/tex}DAO, where O is the mid point of DC?

Solution

Given,
Area of {tex}\triangle{/tex}AFB = Area of parallelogram ABCD
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{2} \times A B \times E F{/tex} = CD {tex}\times{/tex} (Corresponding height)
[{tex}\because{/tex} area of triangle = base {tex}\times{/tex} height and area of parallelogram = base {tex}\times{/tex} corresponding height]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{2} \times A B \times E F=C D \times E G{/tex} 
 
Let the corresponding height be h.
Then, {tex}\frac{1}{2} \times 10 \times 16=10 \times h{/tex} 
[{tex}\because{/tex} altitude, EF = 16 cm and base, AB = 10 cm, given] [{tex}\because{/tex} AB = CD]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} h = 8 cm
In {tex}\triangle{/tex}DAO, DO = 5 cm  [{tex}\because{/tex} O is the mid-point of CD]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Area of {tex}\triangle{/tex}DAO = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \times O D \times h{/tex} 
= {tex}\frac{1}{2} \times 5 \times 8{/tex} = 20 cm2 

Q 76

Ratio of the area of {tex}\triangle{/tex}WXY to the area of {tex}\triangle{/tex}WZY is 3 : 4 (as shown in figure). If the area of {tex}\triangle{/tex}WXZ is 56 cm2 and WY = 8 cm, find the lengths of XY and YZ.

Solution

Given, area of {tex}\triangle{/tex}WXZ = 56 cm2 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{2} \times WY \times XZ{/tex} = 56 [{tex}\because{/tex} area of triangle = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \times base \times height{/tex}]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{2} \times 8 \times X Z{/tex} = 56 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} XZ = 14 cm [{tex}\because{/tex} WY = 8 cm, given]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Area of {tex}\triangle{/tex}WXY : Area of {tex}\triangle{/tex}WZY = 3 : 4
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{\text { Area of } \Delta W XY}{\text { Area of } \Delta W Z Y}=\frac{3}{4}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{\frac{1}{2} \times WY \times X Y}{\frac{1}{2} \times Y Z \times WY}=\frac{3}{4}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{XY}{YZ}=\frac{3}{4}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{XY}{XZ -XY}=\frac{3}{4}{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} YZ = XZ - XY]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{XY}{14-XY}=\frac{3}{4}{/tex}  [By cross-multiplication]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 4XY = 42 - 3XY
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 7XY = 42 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} XY = 6 cm
SO, YZ = XZ - XY = 14 - 6
YZ = 8 cm
Hence, XY = 6 cm and YZ = 8 cm

Q 77

Rani bought a new field that is next to one she already owns (as shown in figure). This field is in the shape of a square of side 70 m. She makes a semi-circular lawn of maximum area in this field.

  1. Find the perimeter of the lawn.
  2. Find the area of the square field excluding the lawn.
Solution
  1. Given, side of a square = 70 m

    From the given figure, the diameter of the semi-circle is the same as the side of a square.
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} Diameter of semi-circle = 70 cm
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} Diameter of semi-circle = side of the square
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} Radius = {tex}\frac{70}{2}{/tex} = 35 m  [{tex}\because{/tex} radius = {tex}\frac{diameter}{2}{/tex}]
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} Perimeter of lawn = {tex}\pi r+2 r=\frac{22}{7} \times 35+2 \times 35{/tex} = 110 + 70 = 180m
  2. Area of square = side {tex}\times{/tex} side = 70 {tex}\times{/tex} 70 = 4900 m2 
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} Required area = Area of the square - Area of the semi-circle
    = 4900 - {tex}\frac{1}{2} \times \pi \times(35)^{2}{/tex}  [{tex}\because{/tex} area of semi-circle = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \pi r^{2}{/tex} 
    = 4900 - {tex}\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{22}{7} \times 35 \times 35{/tex} = 4900 - 1925
    = 2975 m2
Q 78

In the figure, find the area of parallelogram ABCD if the area of the shaded triangle is 9 cm2.

Solution

Area of shaded portion = 9 sq. cm
Base of triangle = 3 cm
Now, the area of a triangle,
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 9 = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 3 {tex}\times{/tex} h
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} h = {tex}\frac{18}{3}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} h = 6 cm
Area of parallelogram = 6 {tex}\times{/tex} (3 + 4) = 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 7 = 42 cm2

Q 79

Pizza factory has come out with two kinds of pizzas. A square pizza of side 45 cm costs ₹150 and a circular pizza of diameter 50 cm costs ₹160 (as shown in figure). Which pizza is a better deal?

Solution

Given, the side of square pizza = 45 cm
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Area of a square pizza = (side)2 = (45)2 = 2025 cm2 
Diameter of circular pizza = 50 cm
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Radius = {tex}\frac{50}{2}{/tex} = 25 cm [{tex}\because{/tex} radius = {tex}\frac{diameter}{2}{/tex}]
Now, area of the circular pizza = {tex}\frac{22}{7} \times 25 \times 25{/tex} 
= {tex}\frac{22}{7} \times 625{/tex} 
= {tex}\frac{13750}{7}{/tex} = 1964.28 cm2 [{tex}\because{/tex} area of circle = {tex}\pi{/tex}r2]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Price of 1 cm square pizza = {tex}\frac{2025}{150}{/tex} = ₹13.5
And price of 1 cm circular pizza = {tex}\frac{1964.28}{160}{/tex} = ₹12.27
Hence, the circular pizza is a better deal

Q 80

Q 81

In a figure, ABCD is a square with AB = 15 cm. Find the area of the square BDFE.

Solution

Given, ABCD is a square and AB = 15 cm
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Diagonal of square ABCD = {tex}\sqrt{2}a{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{2} \times 15=15 \sqrt{2}{/tex} cm
From the figure, diagonal of square ABCD is the side of square BDEF.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Area of the square BDFE = (side)2 = {tex}15 \times 15 \times \sqrt{2} \times \sqrt{2}{/tex} 
= 225 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 = 450 cm2 

Q 82

In the given triangles of the given figure, perimeter of {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC = perimeter of {tex}\triangle{/tex}PQR. Find the area of {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC.
  

Solution

Given, perimeter of {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC = Perimeter of {tex}\triangle{/tex}PQR
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Perimeter of {tex}\triangle{/tex}PQR = 14 + 6 + 10 = 30 cm [{tex}\because{/tex} Perimeter of triangle = sum of all sides]
Now, perimeter of {tex}\triangle{/tex} ABC = AB + BC + CA
30 = AB + BC + AC
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 30 = AB + 5 + 13
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 30 = AB + 18
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} AB = 30 - 18 = 12 cm
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Area of {tex}\triangle{/tex} ABC = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \times {/tex} Base {tex}\times{/tex} Height 
= {tex}\frac{1}{2} \times 5 \times 12{/tex} = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 6 = 30 cm2 

Q 83

Altitudes MN and MO of parallelogram MGHK are 8 cm and 4 cm long respectively (as shown in figure). One side GH is 6 cm long. Find the perimeter of MGHK.

Solution

Given, MGHK is a parallelogram, where MN = 8 cm, MO = 4 cm and GH = 6 cm.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Area of parallelogram MGHK, when base is GH [{tex}\because{/tex} area of parallelogram = base {tex}\times{/tex} height]
= GH {tex}\times{/tex} MN
= 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 8 cm2 = 48 cm2 ...(i)
Area of parallelogram MGHK, when base is HK = HK {tex}\times{/tex} MO 

{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 48 HK {tex}\times{/tex} 4 [from Eq.(i)]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} HK = {tex}\frac{48}{4}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Hk = 12 cm
In parallelogram, opposite sides are equal.
So, GH = MK = 6 cm and MG = HK = 12 cm
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Perimeter of parallelogram MGHK = (6 + 6 = 12 + 12) cm = 36 cm

Q 84

In the given figure, the area of {tex}\triangle{/tex}PQR is 20 cm2 and area of {tex}\triangle{/tex}PQS is 44 cm2. Find the length RS, if PQ is perpendicular to QS and QR is 5 cm.

Solution

Given, area of {tex}\triangle{/tex}PQR = 20 cm2 and area of {tex}\triangle{/tex}PQS = 44 cm2 
We know that,
Area of triangle = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \times {/tex} Base {tex}\times{/tex} Height 
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Area of {tex}\triangle{/tex}PQR = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \times P Q \times Q R{/tex}  [{tex}\because{/tex} PQ {tex}\perp{/tex} QR]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 20 = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \times P Q \times 5{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{20 \times 2}{5}{/tex},= PQ  [{tex}\because{/tex} QR = 5 cm, given]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} PQ = 8 cm
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Area of {tex}\triangle{/tex}PQS = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \times P Q \times Q S{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 44 = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \times 8 \times Q S{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} QS = {tex}\frac{44 \times 2}{8}{/tex}  [{tex}\because{/tex} PQ = 8 cm]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} QS = 11 cm
Now, RS = QS - QR = 11 - 5 = 6 cm

Q 85

Area of an isosceles triangle is 48 cm2. If the altitudes corresponding to the base of the triangle is 8 cm, find the perimeter of the triangle.

Solution


Given, area of {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC = 48 cm2 and altitude = 8 cm
{tex}\because{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC is an isosceles triangle, where AB = AC
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Area of {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \times B C \times A D{/tex} = 48  [{tex}\because{/tex} area of triangle = base {tex}\times{/tex} height]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 48 = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \times B C \times A D{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{2} \times B C \times 8{/tex} = 48 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} BC = {tex}\frac{48 \times 2}{8}{/tex} 
BC = 12 cm
Now, in an isosceles triangle, BD = DC = 6 cm
Applying Pythagoras theorem in right angled {tex}\triangle{/tex}ADB,
AB2 = BD2 + AD2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} AB2 = 62 + 82 = 36 + 64 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} AB2 = 100
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} AB = 10 cm 
Now, perimeter of triangle = AB + AC + BC = AB + AB + BC  [{tex}\because{/tex} AB = AC]
= 10 + 10 + 12
= 32 cm

Q 86

Perimeter of a parallelogram-shaped land is 96 m and its area is 270 square metres. If one of the sides of this parallelogram is 18 m, find the length of the other side. Also, find the lengths of altitudes l and m (as shown in figure).

Solution

Given, perimeter of parallelogram = 96 m and area of parallelogram = 270 m2.
In a parallelogram ABCD, AB = CD = 18 m and AD = BC

As we know, perimeter of a parallelogram ABCD = AB + BC + CD + AD
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 96 = 18 + AD + 18 + AD  [{tex}\because{/tex} AD = BC]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 96 = 36 = 2AD
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2AD = 60 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Ad = 30 cm
So, AD = BC = 30 cm
Now, area of parallelogram ABCD = Base {tex}\times{/tex} Corresponding height
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 270 = AB {tex}\times{/tex} DE [{tex}\because{/tex} base = AB]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 270 = 18 {tex}\times{/tex} DE
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{270}{18}{/tex} = DE 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} DE = 15 m
Also, area of parallelogram ABCD = AD {tex}\times{/tex} BF [{tex}\because{/tex} base = AD]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 270 = 30 {tex}\times{/tex} l 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} l = {tex}\frac{270}{30}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} l = 9 m
Hence, altitude l = 9 m and m = 15 m.

Q 87

Area of a triangle PQR right-angled at Q is 60 cm2 (as shown in figure). If the smallest side is 8cm long, find the length of the other two sides.

Solution

Given, area of {tex}\triangle{/tex}PQR = 60 cm2 and side PQ = 8 cm
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Area of {tex}\triangle{/tex}PQR = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \times P Q \times Q R{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} area of triangle = base {tex}\times{/tex} height]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 60 = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \times 8 \times Q R{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} QR = {tex}\frac{60 \times 2}{8}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} QR = 15 cm 
In right {tex}\triangle{/tex}PQR, PR2 = PQ2 + QR2 [By pythagoras theorem]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} PR2 = 82 + 152 = 64 + 225
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} PR2 = 289 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} PR = {tex}\sqrt{289}{/tex} = 17 cm
Hence, the length of two sides are 15 cm and 17 cm.

Q 88

In the given figure a rectangle with perimeter 264 cm is divided into five congruent rectangles. Find the perimeter of one of the rectangles.

Solution

Let l and b be the length and breadth of each rectangle, respectively.

Given, perimeter of a rectangle = 264 cm
According to the figure, 4l + 5b = 264 ...(i)
and 2l = 3b ...(ii)
Put the value of 3b from eq.(ii) in eq.(i) 2(2l) + 5b = 264 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 3b + 5b = 264 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 6b + 5b = 264 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 11b = 264 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} b = {tex}\frac{264}{11}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} b = 24 cm 
{tex}\therefore{/tex} l = {tex}\frac{3 b}{2}=\frac{3 \times 24}{2}{/tex} = 36 cm
Hence, perimeter of the rectangle = 2(l + b) = 2(36 + 24) = 120 cm

Q 89

Find the area of a square inscribed in a circle whose radius is 7 cm (as shown in figure).
[Hint: Four right-angled triangles joined at right angles to form a square]

Solution


Given that ABCD is a square.
According to the question.
Area of square ABCD = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} Area of a right-angled triangle
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Area of square ABCD = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} Area of {tex}\triangle{/tex}AOB
= {tex}4 \times\left(\frac{1}{2} \times A O \times B O\right){/tex} 
[{tex}\because{/tex} area of a right angled triangle = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \times {/tex} base {tex}\times{/tex} height 
= 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 7 {tex}\times{/tex} 7 = 98 cm2  [{tex}\because{/tex} AO = BO = radius of circle = 7 cm, given]
Hence, the area of inscribed square is 98 cm2

Find the area enclosed in the given figure.

Solution

{tex}\therefore{/tex} Area of rectangle = l {tex}\times{/tex} b = (10.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.5) cm2 = 15.3 cm2

Q 93

Find the area enclosed in the given figure.

Q 94

Find the area enclosed in the given figure.

Q 95

Find the area of the shaded region in the given figure.

Solution

Let radius of smaller circle be r and bigger circle be R.
From the figure, r = {tex}\frac{7}{2}{/tex} cm and R = {tex}\frac{7}{2}+7=\frac{21}{2}{/tex} cm 
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Area of shaded region = Area of bigger circle - Area of smaller circle
= {tex}\pi R^{2}-\pi r^{2}{/tex} 
= {tex}\pi\left(R^{2}-r^{2}\right){/tex} = {tex}\pi\left(\frac{21}{2} \times \frac{21}{2}-\frac{7}{2} \times \frac{7}{2}\right){/tex} 
= {tex}\pi\left(\frac{441}{4}-\frac{49}{4}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{22}{7} \times \frac{392}{4}{/tex} = 308 cm2 
Hence, the area of shaded region is 308 cm2.

Q 96

Find the area of the shaded region in the figure.

Solution

Diameter of complete circle = 14 cm 
Radius = {tex}\frac{14}{2}{/tex} = 7 cm  [{tex}\because{/tex} radius = {tex}\frac{diameter}{2}{/tex}]
So, area of complete circle = {tex}\pi r^{2}=\frac{22}{7} \times 7 \times 7{/tex} = 154 cm2 
Diameter of small circle = {tex}\frac{7}{2}{/tex} cm
Radius = {tex}\frac{7}{4 \times 2}=\frac{7}{8}{/tex} cm 
Area of two small circle = {tex}2 \times \pi^{2}{/tex} = {tex}2 \times \frac{22}{7} \times \frac{7}{8} \times \frac{7}{8}=\frac{77}{16}{/tex} cm2 
Area of shaded region = Area of complete circle - Area of two small circle
= {tex}154-\frac{77}{16}=\frac{154 \times 16-77}{16}{/tex} [taking LCM]
= {tex}\frac{2464-77}{16}=\frac{2387}{16}=149 \frac{3}{16}{/tex} cm2 
Hence, the area of shaded region is {tex}149 \frac{3}{16}{/tex} cm2.

Q 98

A large square is made by arranging a small square surrounded by four congruent rectangles as shown in figure. If the perimeter of each of the rectangle is 16 cm, find the area of the large square.

{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} l = b = 8 cm 
Since, the side of larger square is (l + b).
Hence, area = (side)2 = (l + b)2 = (8)2 = 64 cm2 

Q 99

ABCD is a parallelogram in which AE is perpendicular to CD (as shown in the figure). Also AC = 5 cm, DE = 4 cm, and the area of {tex}\triangle{/tex}AED = 6 cm2. Find the perimeter and area of ABCD.

Solution

Given, area of {tex}\triangle{/tex} AED = 6 cm2 and AC = 5 cm and DE = 4 cm 
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Area of {tex}\triangle{/tex}AED = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \times D E \times A E{/tex}  [{tex}\because{/tex} area of triangle = base {tex}\times{/tex} height]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{2} \times 4 \times A E{/tex} = 6
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} AE = {tex}\frac{6 \times 2}{4}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} AE = 3 cm 
Now, in right angled {tex}\triangle{/tex}AEC, AE = 3 cm and AC = 5 cm 
So, (EC)2 = (AC)2 - (AE)2 [By pythagoras theorem]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (EC)2 = 52 - 32 = 25 - 9 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} EC = {tex}\sqrt{16}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} EC = 4 cm 
{tex}\because{/tex} DE + EC = DC
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} DC = 4 + 4 = 8 cm 
{tex}\because{/tex} ABCD is a parallelogram.
So, AB = DC = 8 cm 
Now, in right angled {tex}\triangle{/tex}AED, AD2 = AE2 + ED2 [By pythagoras theorem]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} AD2 = 32 + 42 = 9 + 16 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} AD = {tex}\sqrt{25}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} AD = 5 cm 
So, AD = BC = 5 cm  [{tex}\because{/tex} ABCD is a parallelogram]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Perimeter of parallelogram ABCD = 2(l + b) = 2(DC + AD) = 2(8 + 5) = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 12 = 26 cm
Area of parallelogram ABCD = Base {tex}\times{/tex} Height = DC {tex}\times{/tex} AE = 8 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 = 24 cm2 

Q 100

Ishika has designed a small oval race track for her remote control car. Her design is shown in the figure. What is the total distance around the track? Round your answer to the nearest whole cm.

Solution

Total distance around the track = Length of 2 parallel strips + Length of 2 semi-circles
= 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 52 + {tex}2 \times \pi \times 16{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} r = 16 cm]
= 104 + {tex}2 \times 3.14 \times 16{/tex} 
= 104 + 100.5009
= 205 cm (approx.)

Q 101

A table cover of dimensions 3 m 25 cm {tex}\times{/tex} 2 m 30 cm is spread on a table. If 30 cm of the table cover is hanging all around the table, find the area of the table cover which is hanging outside the top of the table. Also, find the cost of polishing the table top at ₹16 per square metre.

Solution

To find the cost of polishing the tabletop, we have to find its area for which we require its length and breadth.
Given, length of cover = 3 m 25 cm = 3.25 m and breadth of cover = 2 m 30 cm = 2.30 m
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Area of the table cover = 3.25 {tex}\times{/tex} 2.30 = 7.475 m2 
Since 30 cm width of the cloth is outside the table on each side.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Length of the table = 3.25 - 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.30 = 2.65 m [{tex}\because{/tex} 1 cm = {tex}\frac{1}{100}{/tex} m]
and breadth of the table = 2.30 - 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.30 = 1.70 m
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Area of the top of the table = (2.65 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.70) m2 
= 4.505 m2 
Area of the hanging table cover = Area of table cover - Area of the top of the table
= (7.475 - 4.505) m2 
= 2.97 m2 

It is given that, the cost of polishing the tabletop is at the rate of ₹16 per square meter.
Therefore, the cost of polishing the top = Area {tex}\times{/tex} Rate per square meter
= 4.505 {tex}\times{/tex} 16
= ₹7.208

Q 102

The dimensions of a plot are 200 m {tex}\times{/tex} 150 m. A builder builds 3 roads which are 3 m wide along the length on either side and one in the middle. On either side of the middle road he builds houses to sell. How much area did he get for building the houses?

Solution

Given that, dimensions of piot = 200m {tex}\times{/tex} 150 m and width of road = 3m 

{tex}\therefore{/tex} Total area available for houses = Area of total plot - Area of 3 roads
= 200 {tex}\times{/tex} 150 - 3 {tex}\times{/tex} (3 {tex}\times{/tex} 200) [{tex}\because{/tex} area of rectangle = length {tex}\times{/tex} breadth]
= 30000 - 1800 = 28200 m2

Q 103

A room is 4.5 m long and 4 m wide. The floor of the room is to be covered with tiles of size 15 cm by 10 cm. Find the cost of covering the floor with tiles at the rate of ₹4.50 per tile.

Solution

Given, length of room = 4.5 m, width of room = 4 m and size of tiles = 15 cm {tex}\times{/tex} 10 cm 
{tex}\because{/tex} Area of room = l {tex}\times{/tex} b = 4.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 4 = 18 m2 = 18 {tex}\times{/tex} (100)2 cm2 = 180000 cm2 [{tex}\because{/tex} 1 m = 100 cm]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Area of 1 tile = 15 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 = 150 cm2 
So, number of tiles = {tex}\frac{\text { Area of } \mathrm{room}}{\text { Area of } 1 \text { tile }}=\frac{180000}{150}{/tex} = 1200
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Cost of covering the floor with tiles = ₹4.50 {tex}\times{/tex} 1200 = ₹5400

Q 104

Find the total cost of wooden fencing around a circular garden of diameter 28 m, if 1m of fencing costs ₹300.

Solution

Given, diameter of a circular garden = 28 m
Length of the fencing = Circumference of circle
= {tex}\pi d=\frac{22}{7} \times 28{/tex} = 8 m
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Total cost of fencing = 88 {tex}\times{/tex} 300 = ₹26400

Q 105

Priyanka took a wire and bent it to form a circle of radius 14 cm. Then she bent it into a rectangle with one side 24 cm long. What is the length of the wire? Which figure encloses more area, the circle, or the rectangle?

Solution

Given that, radius of circle (r) = 14 cm and length of rectangle (l) = 24 cm 
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Length of the wire = Circumference of the circle = {tex}2 \pi r=2 \times \frac{22}{7} \times 14{/tex} = 88 cm 
Let b be the width of rectangle.
Since the wire is rebent in the form of rectangle.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Perimeter of rectangle = Circumference of circle
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2(24 + b) = 88 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 24 + b = 44 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} b = 44 - 24 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} b = 20 cm 
Area of the circle = {tex}\pi r^{2}=\frac{22}{7} \times(14)^{2}{/tex} = 616 cm2 
{tex}\because{/tex} Area of rectangle = l {tex}\times{/tex} b = 24 {tex}\times{/tex} 20 = 480 cm2 
Hence, the circle encloses more area than rectangle.

Q 106

How much distance, in metres, a wheel of 25 cm radius will cover if it rotates 350 times?

Solution

Given, radius of wheel (r) = 25 cm = {tex}\frac{25}{100} m=\frac{1}{4} m{/tex}   [{tex}\because{/tex} 1 cm = {tex}\frac{1}{100}{/tex} m]
Distance travelled in one rotation = {tex}2 \pi r=2 \times \frac{22}{7} \times \frac{1}{4}=\frac{11}{7} \mathrm{m}{/tex} 
Distance travelled in 350 rotation = {tex}\frac{11}{7} \times 350{/tex} = 550 m 
Hence, the wheel coveres 550m distance.

Q 107

A circular pond is surrounded by a 2 m wide circular path. If the outer circumference of the circular path is 44 m, find the inner circumference of the circular path. Also, find area of the path.

Solution

Let R and r be the radius of outer circle and inner circle, respectively.

It is given that, the circumference of outer circle is 44 m.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 2{tex}\pi{/tex}R = 44 [{tex}\because{/tex} circumference of circle = 2{tex}\pi{/tex}l]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}2 \times \frac{22}{7} \times R{/tex} = 44
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} R = {tex}\frac{44}{2 \times \frac{22}{7}}=\frac{7 \times 44}{2 \times 22}{/tex} = 7 m 
Since, r = (R - 2)m = (7 - 2)m = 5m 
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Inner circumference of the circular path = {tex}2 \pi r=2 \times \frac{22}{7} \times 5{/tex} = 31.43 m (approx.)
{tex}\because{/tex} Area of path = Area of outer circle - Area of inner circle = {tex}\pi{/tex}(R2 - r2)
= {tex}\frac{22}{7}\left(7^{2}-5^{2}\right)=\frac{22}{7} \times 24{/tex} = 75.43 m2 (approx.)

Q 108

A carpet of size 5 m {tex}\times{/tex} 2 m has 25 cm wide red border. The inner part of the carpet is blue in colour (as shown in the figure). Find the area of blue portion. What is the ratio of areas of red portion to blue portion?

Solution

Given, size of carpet = 5m {tex}\times{/tex} 2m and width of border = 25 cm = {tex}\frac{25}{100}{/tex}m = 0.25 m 

Area of carpet ABCD = AB {tex}\times{/tex} BC = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 = 10 m2 [{tex}\because{/tex} area of rectangle = length {tex}\times{/tex} breadth]
So, length of inner blue portion, EF = AB - (2 {tex} \times{/tex} 0.25 cm) = 5 - 0.50 = 4.5 m and breadth of inner blue portion FG = BC - (2 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.25) = 2 - 0.50 = 1.5 m 
Area of blue portion = Area of rectangle EFGH = EF{tex}\times{/tex} FG = 45 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.5 = 675 m2 
Now, area of red portion = Area of ABCD - Area of EFGH = 10 - 675 = 3.25 m2
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Ratio of areas of red portion to blue portion = 3.25 : 6.75 = 13 : 27

Q 109

Use the given figure showing the layout of a farm house:

  1. What is the area of land used to grow hay?
  2. It costs ₹91 per m2 to fertilise the vegetable garden. What is the total cost?
  3. A fence is to be enclosed around the house. The dimensions of the house are 18.7 m {tex}\times{/tex} 12.6 m. At least how many metres of fencing are needed?
  4. Each banana tree required 1.25 m2 of ground space. How many banana trees can there be in the orchard?
     
Solution
  1. Area of land used to grow hay = 17.8 m {tex}\times{/tex} 10.6 m = 188.68 m2 [{tex}\because{/tex} area of rectangle = length {tex}\times{/tex} breadth]
  2. Area of vegetable garden = 49 m {tex}\times{/tex} 15.2 m = 744.80 m2 
    Cost of fertilise 1 m2 vegetable garden = ₹91 
    Cost to fertilise 744.80 m2 vegetable garden = 91 {tex}\times{/tex} 744.80 = 67776.80 
  3. Since, fence is to be enclosed around the house of dimensions 18.7 m {tex}\times{/tex} 12.6 m
    Perimeter of the house = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} (l + b)
    Total length of fence = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} (18.7 + 12.6)m = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 31.3 m = 62.6 m
  4. Area covered vy banana orchard = 20 m {tex}\times{/tex} 15.7 m = 314 m2 
    Since 1.25 m2 area is required by 1 banana tree.
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} 314 m2 area is required by number of banana trees = {tex}\frac{314}{1.25}{/tex} = 251.25 = 251 trees
Q 110

Study the layout given below in figure and answer the questions:

  1. Write an expression for the total area covered by both the bedrooms and the kitchen.
  2. Write an expression to calculate the perimeter of the living room.
  3. If the cost of carpeting is ₹50/m2, write an expression for calculating the total cost of carpeting both the bedrooms and the living room.
  4. If the cost of tiling is ₹30/m2, write an expression for calculating the total cost of floor tiles used for the bathroom and kitchen floors.
  5. If the floor area of each bedroom is 35 m2, then find x.
Solution
  1. Area of both bedrooms and the kitchen = (Area of bedroom) {tex}\times{/tex} 2 + Area of kitchen
    = (5 {tex}\times{/tex} x)2 + 15 - (x + 2) {tex}\times{/tex} 5
    = 10x + (75 - 5x - 10) = 10x + 65 - 5x
    = (65 + 5x)m2 
  2. Perimeter of the living room = 15 + 2 + 5 + (15 - x) + 5 + x + 2 = 44 m
  3. Total area of both the bedrooms and the living room = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} x + 7 {tex}\times{/tex} 15 = (5x + 105)m2 
    Total cost of carpeting = (5x + 105) {tex}\times{/tex} 50 = ₹250(x + 21)
  4. Total area of bethroom and kitchen = (15 - x) {tex}\times{/tex} 5 m2 
    Total cost of tiling = (15 - x) {tex}\times{/tex} 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 30 = 150(15 - x)
  5. Given, area of floor of each bedroom = 35 m2 
    Area of one bedroom = 5x m2 
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} 5x = 35 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 7 m
Q 111

A 10 m long and 4 m wide rectangular lawn is in front of a house. Along its three sides, a 50 cm wide flower bed is there as shown in the figure. Find the area of the remaining portion.

Solution

Given, dimensions of rectangular lawn = 10 m {tex}\times{/tex} 4 m and width of flower bed = 50 cm

Length of remaining portion, EF = AB - (50 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 cm)
= 10 m - 100 cm = 10 m - 1m = 9 m [{tex}\because{/tex} 1 cm = {tex}\frac{1}{100}{/tex} m]
Breadth of remaining portion, EH = AD - 50 cm = 4m - 0.5m = 3.5 m
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Required area = Area of portion EFGH = EF {tex}\times{/tex} EH = 9 {tex}\times{/tex} 3.5 = 31.5 m2 

Q 112

A school playground is divided by a 2 m wide path which is parallel to the width of the playground, and a 3 m wide path which is parallel to the length of the ground (as shown in the figure). If the length and width of the playground are 120 m and 80 m respectively, find the area of the remaining playground.

Solution

Given, dimensions of playground = 120 m {tex}\times{/tex} 80 m 

Area of rectangle = 120 {tex}\times{/tex} 80 = 9600 m2 
Area of rectangle ABFE = AB {tex}\times{/tex} BF = 120 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 = 360 m2 [{tex}\because{/tex} area of rectangle = length {tex}\times{/tex} breadth]
Area of rectangle GHIJ = JI {tex}\times{/tex} IH = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 77 = 154 m2 
Area of remaining ground rectangle GHIJ = Area of rectangle ABCD - Area of rectangle ABFE - Area of GHIJ
= 9600 - 360 - 154 = 9086 m2 

Q 113

In a park of dimensions 20 m {tex}\times{/tex} 15 m, there is a L shaped 1m wide flower bed as shown in the figure. Find the total cost of manuring for the flower bed at the rate of ₹45 per m2.

Solution

Given, dimensions of a park = 20 m {tex}\times{/tex} 15m and width of a flower bed = 1m

From the figure, FG = BC - 1m = (15 - 1) = 14 m 
EF = DC - 1 m = (20 - 1) = 19 m 
Area of flower bed = Area of ABCD - Area of EFGD 
= 20 {tex}\times{/tex} 15 - 19 {tex}\times{/tex} 14 = 300 - 266 = 34 m2 [{tex}\because{/tex} area of rectangle = length {tex}\times{/tex} breadth]
Cost of manuring 1 m2 of flower bed = ₹45
Cost of manuring 34 m2 the flower bed = ₹34 {tex}\times{/tex} 45 = ₹1530

Q 114

Dimensions of a painting are 60 cm {tex}\times{/tex} 38 cm. Find the area of the wooden frame of width 6 cm around the painting as shown in the figure.
 

Solution

We have,
Length and breadth of inner rectangle is 60 cm and 38 cm, respectively.
Area of inner rectangle = 60 {tex}\times{/tex} 38 = 2280 cm2 [{tex}\because{/tex} area of rectangle = length {tex}\times{/tex} breadth]
Breadth of outer rectangle = 38 + 6 + 6 = 50 cm 
Length of outer rectangle = 60 + 6 + 6 = 72 cm [{tex}\because{/tex} width of frame = 6 cm, given]
Area of outer rectangle = 50 {tex}\times{/tex} 72 = 3600 cm2 
Now, area of wooden frame = 3600 - 2280 = 1320 cm2 

Q 115

A design is made up of four congruent right triangles as shown in figure. Find the area of the shaded portion.

Solution


Area of one right angled triangle = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \times BH \times B G{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \times 10 \times 30{/tex} = 150 cm2 
So, area of 4 right angled triangles = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 150 = 600 cm2  [{tex}\because{/tex} all the right angled triangles are congruent]
Area of square = (side)2 [GC = 10 cm, because all the riangles are congruent]
Area of portion ABCD = (30 + 10)2 = 402 = 1600 cm2 
Area of shaded portion = 1600 - 600 = 1000 cm2 

Q 116

A square tile of length 20 cm has four quarter circles at each corner as shown in figure(i). Find the area of the shaded portion. Another tile with the same dimensions has a circle in the centre of the tile [as shown in figure (ii)]. If the circle touches all the four sides of the square tile, find the area of the shaded portion. In which tile, area of the shaded portion will be more? (Take {tex}\pi{/tex} = 3.14)
  

Solution
  1. Area of shaded portion = Area of square - 4 {tex}\times{/tex} Area of quarter circle
    = {tex}20 \times 20-4 \times \frac{\pi r^{2}}{4}{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} area of square = (side)2 and area of quarter circle = {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex}area of a circle]
    = {tex}400-4 \times \frac{22}{7} \times \frac{1}{4} \times 10 \times 10{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} radius of quarter circle = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} side of square = 10 cm]
    = {tex}400-\frac{2200}{7}=\frac{600}{7}{/tex} = 85.71 cm2 = 86 cm2 
  2. Area of shaded portion = Area of square - Area of circle
    = {tex}20 \times 20-\frac{22}{7} \times 10 \times 10{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} radius of circle = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} side of square = 10 cm]
    = {tex}400-\frac{2200}{7}=\frac{600}{7}{/tex} = 86 cm2
    Hence, area in both cases is equal i.e. 86 cm2.
Q 117

A rectangular field is 48 m long and 12 m wide. How many right triangular flower beds can be laid in this field, if sides including the right angle measure 2 m and 4 m, respectively?

Solution

Given, dimensions of a rectangular field = 48 m {tex}\times{/tex} 12 m and dimensions of a right-angled triangle = 2 m {tex}\times{/tex} 4 m
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Number of right triangular flower beds = {tex}\frac{\text { Area of field }}{\text { Area of right angled triangle }}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{48 \times 12}{\frac{1}{2} \times 2 \times 4}{/tex} 
= 144 [{tex}\because{/tex} area of rectangle = length {tex}\times{/tex} breadth and area of a right-angled triangle = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \times{/tex} height {tex}\times{/tex} breadth]

Q 118

Ramesh grew wheat in a rectangular field that measured 32 metres long and 26 metres wide. This year he increased the area for wheat by increasing the length but not the width. He increased the area of the wheat field by 650 square metres. What is the length of the expanded wheat field?

Solution

Given, dimensions of a rectangular field 32 m {tex}\times{/tex} 26 m and area increased = 650 m2 

Increased area of wheat field = Area of EFGH - Area of ABCD wheat field
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 650 = EF {tex}\times{/tex} EH - AB {tex}\times{/tex} AD [{tex}\because{/tex} area of rectangle = length {tex}\times{/tex} breadth]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 650 = EF {tex}\times{/tex} 26 - 32 {tex}\times{/tex} 26 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 650 = 26EF - 832 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 1482 = 26EF {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} EF = 57 m 
Hence, the length of the expanded wheat field is 57 m.

Q 119

In a figure, triangle AEC is right-angled at E, B is a point on EC, BD is the altitude of triangle ABC, AC = 25 cm, BC = 7 cm and AE = 15 cm. Find the area of triangle ABC and the length of DB.

Solution

Given, AC = 25 cm, BC = 7 cm, and AE = 15 cm 
In {tex}\triangle{/tex}AEC, using pythagoras theorem,
AC2 = AE2 + EC2 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} EC2 = AC2 - AE2 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} EC2 = (25)2 - (15)2 = 625 - 225 
= 400 
EC = {tex}\sqrt{400}{/tex} = 20 cm 
and EB = EC - BC = 20 - 7 = 13 cm 
Area of {tex}\triangle{/tex}AEC = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \times A E \times E C{/tex} 
= {tex}\frac{1}{2} \times 15 \times 20{/tex} = 150 cm2 
and Area of {tex}\triangle{/tex}AEB = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \times A E \times E B=\frac{1}{2} \times 15 \times 13{/tex} = 97.5 cm2 
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Area of {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC = Area of {tex}\triangle{/tex}AEC - Area of {tex}\triangle{/tex}AEB
= 150 - 97.5 = 52.5 cm2 
Again, Area of {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \times {/tex} BD {tex}\times{/tex} AC 
52.5 = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \times {/tex} BD {tex}\times{/tex} 25
BD = {tex}\frac{52.5 \times 2}{25}{/tex} = 4.2 cm 
Hence, the area of {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC is 52.5 cm2 and the length of DB is 4.2 cm.

Q 120

Solution

Number of pieces of chocolate = {tex}\frac{\text{Area of sheet of chocolate}}{\text{Area of one chocolate}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{18 \times 18}{1.5 \times 2}=\frac{324}{3}{/tex} = 108

Q 121

Calculate the area of the shaded region in a figure, where all of the short line segments are at right angles to each other and 1 cm long.

Solution


Length of the larger rectangle = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 9 cm = 9 cm
Breadth of the larger rectangle =1 {tex}\times{/tex} 9 cm = 9 cm
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Area of shaded region = Area of larger square - Area of 41 small identical square
= 9 {tex}\times{/tex} 9 - 41 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 = 81 - 41 = 40 cm2

Q 122

The plan and measurement for a house are given in a figure. The house is surrounded by a path 1m wide.

Find the following:

  1. Cost of paving the path with bricks at a rate of ₹120 per m2.
  2. Cost of wooden flooring inside the house except for the bathroom at the cost of ₹1200 per m2.
  3. Area of Living Room.
Solution

  1. Area of path = Area of rectngle PQRS - Area of rectangle ADFH
    = PQ {tex}\times{/tex} QR - AD {tex}\times{/tex} DF
    = (4 + 2.5 + 4 + 1 + 1) {tex}\times{/tex} (3 + 3 + 1 + 1) - (4 + 2.5 + 4) {tex}\times{/tex} (3 + 3)
    = 12.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 8 - 10.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 6
    = 37 m2 
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} Cost of paving the path with bricks
    = Cost per unit m2 {tex}\times{/tex} Total area of path
    = 120 {tex}\times{/tex} 27
    = 4440 
  2. Area of house except bathroom = Area of house - Area of bathroom
    = Area of rectangle ADFH - Area of rectangle BCKL
    = (4 + 2.5 + 4) {tex}\times{/tex} (3 + 3) - 2.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 2
    = 63 - 5 = 58 m2 
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} Cost of flouring = Cost per unit m2 {tex}\times{/tex} Total area
    = 1200 {tex}\times{/tex} 58 = 69600
  3. Area of living room = Area of rectangle ACGH - Area of rectangle ABJI - Area of rectangle BCLK
    = (4 + 2.5) {tex}\times{/tex} (3 + 3) - 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 - 2.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 = 39 - 12 - 5 = 22m2  
Q 123

Architects design many types of buildings. They draw plans for houses, such as the plan shown in figure:

An architect wants to install decorative moulding around the ceilings in all the rooms. The decorative moulding costs ₹500/metre.
Find how much moulding will be needed for each room.

  1. family room
  2. living room
  3. dining room
  4. bedroom 1
  5. bedroom 2
Solution
  1. Given, breadth of the family room =5 .48 m and length of the family room =4.57 m
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} Perimeter of the family room = 2(Length + Breadth) = 2(5.48 + 4.57)
    = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10.05 = 20.10 m
  2. Given, length of the living room = 3.81 m and breadth of the living room = 7.53 m 
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} Perimeter of the living room = 2(Length + Breadth) = 293.81 + 7.53) 
    = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 11.34 = 22.68 m
  3. Given, breadth of the dining room = 5.48 m and length of the dining room = 5.41 m 
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} Perimeter of dinning room = 2(Length + Breadth) = 2(5.41 + 5.48)
    = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10.89 = 21.78 m 
  4. Given, length of bedroom 1 = 3.04 m and breadth of bedroom 1 = 3.04 m 
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} Perimeter of bedroom = 2(Length + Breadth) = 2(3.04 + 3.04)
    = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 6.08 = 12.16 m
  5. Given, breadth of bedroom 2 = 2.43 m and length of bedroom 2 = 3.04 m 
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} Perimeter of the bedroom 2 = 2(Length + Breadth) = 2(3.04 + 2.43)
    = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 5.47 = 10.94 m 
Q 124

Architects design many types of buildings. They draw plans for houses, such as the plan shown in figure:

An architect wants to install a decorative moulding around the ceilings in all the rooms. The decorative moulding costs ₹500/metre.
The carpet costs ₹200/m2. Find the cost of carpeting each room.

Solution

For bedroom 1, 
Given, length of bedroom 1 = 3.04 m and breadth of bedroom 1 = 3.04 m
Area of bedroom 1 = Length {tex}\times{/tex} Breadth 
Area of bedroom 1 = 3.04 {tex}\times{/tex} 3.04 = 9.2416 sq m 
Cost of carpeting 1 sq m = ₹200 
Cost of carpeting 9.2416 m2 = 9.2416 {tex}\times{/tex} 200 = ₹1848 
For bedroom 2,
Given, length of bedroom 2 = 3.04 m and breadth of bedroom 2 = 2.43 m
Area of bedroom 2 = Length {tex}\times{/tex} Breadth 
Area of bedroom 2 = 3.04 {tex}\times{/tex} 2.43 = 7.3872 sq m2
Cost of carpeting 1 m2 = ₹200 
Cost of carpeting 7.3872 m2 = 7.3872 {tex}\times{/tex} 200 = ₹1477
For living room,
Given, length of living room = 3.81 m and breadth of living room = 7.53 m 
Area of living room = 3.81 {tex}\times{/tex} 7.53 = 28.6893 m2 
Cost of carpeting of living room 1 m2 = ₹200 
Cost of carpeting 28.6893 m2 = ₹200 {tex}\times{/tex} 28.6893 = ₹5737.86
For dining room, 
Given, length of dining room = 5.41 m and breadth of dining room = 5.48 m 
Area of dining room = 5.41 {tex}\times{/tex} 5.48 = 29.6468 m2 
Cost of carpeting 1 m2 = ₹200 
Cost of carpeting 29.6468 m2 = 29.6468 {tex}\times{/tex} 200 = ₹5929.36
For family room,
Given, length of family room = 4.57 m and breadth of family room = 5.48 m 
Area of family room = 5.48 {tex}\times{/tex} 4.57 = 25.0436 m2 
So, cost of carpeting family room = 25.0436 {tex}\times{/tex} 200 = ₹5008.72 

Q 125

Architects design many types of buildings. They draw plans for houses, such as the plan shown in figure:

An architect wants to install decorative moulding around the ceilings in all the rooms. The decorative moulding costs ₹500/metre.
What is the total cost of moulding for all the five rooms?

Solution

Total perimeter of all the five rooms = 20.10 m + 22.68 m + 21.78 m+ 12.16 m + 10.94 m = 87.66 m 
Given cost of moulding each room = ₹500 per m
Total cost of moulding all five rooms = 87.66 {tex}\times{/tex} 500 = ₹43830

Q 126

ABCD is a given rectangle with length as 80 cm and breadth as 60 cm. P, Q, R, S are the mid points of sides AB, BC, CD, DA respectively. A circular rangoli of radius 10 cm is drawn at the centre as shown in figure. Find the area of shaded portion.

Solution

Here, AP = {tex}\frac{1}{2} A B=\frac{1}{2} \times 80{/tex} = 40 cm
Also, AS = {tex}\frac{1}{2} A D=\frac{1}{2} \times 60{/tex} = 30 cm
Area of {tex}\triangle{/tex} APS = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \times A P \times A S=\frac{1}{2} \times 40 \times 30{/tex} = 600 cm2 
Area of portion PQRS = Area of rectangle ABCD - 4 {tex}\times{/tex} Area of {tex}\triangle{/tex}APS = 80 {tex}\times{/tex} 60 - 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 600 
= 4800 - 2400 = 2400 cm2
Area of circular rangoli = {tex}\pi \times(10)^{2}=\frac{22}{7} \times 100{/tex} = 314 cm2 [{tex}\because{/tex} radius of circle = 10 cm]
Area of shaded region = 2400 - 314 = 2086 cm2

Q 127

4 squares each of side 10 cm have been cut from each corner of a rectangular sheet of paper of size 100 cm {tex}\times{/tex} 80 cm. From the remaining piece of paper, an isosceles right triangle is removed whose equal sides are each of 10 cm length. Find the area of the remaining part of the paper.

Solution

Area of each square = (10)2 cm2 = 100 cm2  [∵ area of square = (side)2]
Area of rectangular sheet = 100 {tex}\times{/tex} 80 cm2  [∵ area of rectangle = length {tex}\times{/tex} breadth]
= 8000 cm2 
Area of an isosceles right triangle = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \times {/tex} 10 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 = 50 cm2 [{tex}\because{/tex} area of an isosceles right triangle = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \times base \times height{/tex}]
Area of remaining part of paper = 8000 - 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 100 - 50 
= 7550 cm2

Q 128

A dinner plate is in the form of a circle. A circular region encloses a beautiful design as shown in the figure. The inner circumference is 352 mm and outer is 396 mm. Find the width of circular design.

Solution

Let the radius of the inner and outer circle be r and R, respectively.
Given, inner circumference = 352 mm 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2{tex}\pi{/tex}r = 352 [{tex}\because{/tex} circumference = 2{tex}\pi{/tex}l]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}2 \times \frac{22}{7} \times r{/tex} = 352 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} r = {tex}\frac{352 \times 7}{2 \times 22}=\frac{2464}{44}{/tex} = 56 mm 
and outer circumference = 396 mm 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2{tex}\pi{/tex}r = 396 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}2 \times \frac{22}{7} \times R{/tex} = 396 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} R = {tex}\frac{396 \times 7}{2 \times 22}{/tex} = 63 mm 
Width of circular design = R - r = 63 - 56 = 7 mm

Q 129

The moon is about 384000 km from the earth and its path around the earth is nearly circular. Find the length of the path described by the moon in one complete revolution. (Take {tex}\pi{/tex} = 3.14)

Solution

Length of path described by moon in one complete revolution = 2{tex}\pi{/tex}r
= 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 3.14 {tex}\times{/tex} 384000 [{tex}\because{/tex} radius = distance of moon from the earth]
= 2411520 km

Q 130

A photograph of the Billiard/Snooker table has dimensions as {tex}\frac{1}{10}{/tex}th of its actual size as shown in the figure:

The portion excluding six holes each of diameter 0.5 cm needs to be polished at the rate of ₹200 per m2. Find the cost of polishing.

Solution

Actual length = 25 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 = 250 cm 
Actual breadth = 10 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 = 100 cm 
Area of table = 250 {tex}\times{/tex} 100 = 25000 cm2 
Radius of 1 hole = {tex}\frac{0.5}{2}{/tex} = 0.25 cm 
Area of 6 holes = {tex}6 \times \pi r^{2}=6 \times \frac{22}{7} \times {/tex} 0.25 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.25 = 1.18 cm2 
Area of portion excluding holes = 25000 - 1.18 = 24998.8 cm2 
Cost of polishing = ₹{tex}\frac{24999}{10000} \times 200{/tex} = ₹500 (approx.)

Q 11

Twice the sum of length x and breadth y of a rectangle is the perimeter of a rectangle. Write the expression for perimeter.

Solution

Perimeter of rectangle = 2(Length + Breadth) = 2(x + y) = 2x + 2y

Q 12

Identify the term containing u2 in the expression u3 + 3u2v + 3uv2 + v3 and write its coefficient.

Solution

Term containing u2 = 3u2v
Coefficient of u2 = 3v

Q 13

Simplify the expression by combining the like terms: 7x3 - 3x2y + xy2 + x2y - y3.

Solution

Rearranging the terms in the given expression, we get
7x3 - 3x2y + x2y + xy2 - y3
= 7x3 + (-3x2y) + x2y + xy2 - y3
= 7x3 + (-3 + 1) x2y + xy2 - y3 [Using distributive property]
= 7x3 + (-2) x2y + xy2 - y3
= 7x3 - 2x2y + xy2 - y3

Q 14

Subtract the sum of - 3x3y2 + 2x2y3 and - 3x2y3 - 5y4 from x4 + x3 y2 + x2y3 + y4

Solution


Sum = - 3x3y2 - x2y3 - 5y4

Q 15

Find the value of the given expression at a = 1 and b = -2:
a2 + b2 + 3ab

Solution

Value of a2 + b2 + 3ab at a = 1 and b = - 2
= (1)2 + (- 2)2 + 3(1)(- 2)
= 1 + 4 - 6
= 5 - 6
= -1

Q 16

Find the value of the given expression at a = 1 and b = -2:
a3 + a2b + ab2 + b3

Solution

Value of a3 + a2b + ab2 + b3 at a = 1 and b = - 2
= (1)3 + (1)2(- 2) + (1) (- 2)2 + (- 2)3
= 1 - 2 + 4 - 8
= 5 - 10
= -5

Q 17

Find each side of an equilateral triangle given below, if it’s perimeter is 240 cm.

Solution

3 {tex}\times{/tex} length of one side = perimeter
Therefore, 3 {tex}\times{/tex} (2x + 3) = 240
Thus, 2x + 3 = {tex}\frac{240}{3}{/tex} = 80
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x + 3 = 80
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x = 80 - 3
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x = 77
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex}\frac{77}{2}{/tex}
Therefore, x = 38.5
Hence, Side = 2x + 3 = (2 {tex}\times{/tex} 38.5) + 3 = 80 cm

Sum or difference of two like terms is a like term,
For example, take two like terms 138x2y and 125x2y.
Difference of like terms is = 138x2y - 125x2y = 13x2y, is a like terms.

Q 18

In the formula, area of circle = {tex}\pi{/tex}r2, the numerical constant of the expression {tex}\pi{/tex}r2 is ________.

Solution

In the formula, area of circle = {tex}\pi{/tex}r2, the numerical constant of the expression {tex}\pi{/tex}r2 is r2.

Q 27

If (x2y + y2 + 3) is subtracted from (3x2y + 2y2 + 5), then coefficient of y in the result is ________.

Solution

According to question
=(3x2y + 2y2 + 5) -  (x2y + y2 + 3) 
= 3x2y + 2y2 + 5 - x2y - y2 - 3
= 2x2y + y2 + 2
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Coefficient of y = 2x2

Q 28

- a - b - c is same as - a - (________)

Q 29

The unlike terms in perimeters of following figures are ________ and ________.

Solution

Unlike terms in perimeters are 2y and 2y2.
As we know the addition of all Sides of the given figure is called a perimeter.
From the Fig. (i),
Perimeter = Sum of all sides
= 2x + y + 2x + y = 4x + 2y
From the fig. (ii),
Perimeter = Sum of all sides
= x + y2 + x + y2 = 2x + 2y2

Q 30

On adding a monomial ________ to - 2x + 4y2 + z, the resulting expression becomes a binomial.

Solution

If we added 2x, OR - 4y2 OR - z to the given expression. It becomes a binomial expression
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x + (- 2x + 4y2 + z) = 4y2 + z
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} - 4y2 + (- 2x + 4y2 + z) = - 2x + z
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} - z + (- 2x + 4y2 + z) = - 2x + 4y2
Hence, on adding a monomial 2x or - 4y2 or - z to - 2x + 4y2 + z, the resulting expresion becomes a binomial expression.

Q 31

3x + 23x2 + 6y2 + 2x + y2 + ________ = 5x + 7y2.

Solution

Let (3x + 23x2 + 6y2 + 2x + y2) + M = 5x + 7y2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} M = (5x + 7y2) - (3x + 23x2 + 6y2 + 2x + y2)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} M = 5x + 7y2 - 3x - 23x2 - 6y2 - 2x - y2 ...[With -ve sign, +ve sign in the bracket will change on opening it]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} M = 5x - 3x - 2x + 7y2 - 6y2 - y2 - 23x2
M = 0 + 0 - 23x2 = - 23x2

5a and 5b are unlike terms.

Subtracting a term from a given expression is the same as adding its additive inverse to the given expression.

The total number of planets of Sun can be denoted by the variable n.

In like terms, the numerical coefficients should also be the same.

If we add a monomial and binomial, then the answer can never be a monomial.

Q 50

If we subtract a monomial from a binomial, then the answer is at least a binomial.

When we subtract a monomial from a trinomial, then the answer can be a polynomial.
